title: Risk and Return Fundamentals
description: Understand the relationship between risk and expected return

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Risk and return are foundational to all financial decisions. CMAs must understand how to quantify risk, how investors demand compensation for bearing it, and how to balance risk and return in capital budgeting, valuation, and performance evaluation.

sections.title: Types of Investment Risk
sections.content:
**Systematic Risk (Market Risk):**
• Affects all securities in market
• Cannot be diversified away
• Examples: Interest rates, inflation, recessions
• Measured by beta

**Unsystematic Risk (Firm-Specific):**
• Unique to individual company
• CAN be diversified away
• Examples: Management changes, lawsuits, product recalls

**Total Risk = Systematic + Unsystematic**

**Key insight:** Investors shouldn't expect extra return for unsystematic risk because it's diversifiable!

sections.title: Measuring Return
sections.content:
**Holding Period Return:**
HPR = (Ending Price - Beginning Price + Dividends) / Beginning Price

**Example:**
Bought at $100, sold at $112, received $4 dividend
HPR = ($112 - $100 + $4) / $100 = **16%**

**Expected Return:**
E(R) = Σ [Probability × Return]

**Example:**
40% chance of 20% return, 60% chance of 8% return
E(R) = (0.40 × 20%) + (0.60 × 8%) = **12.8%**

sections.title: Measuring Risk: Standard Deviation
sections.content:
**Standard Deviation (σ):**
Measures dispersion of returns around the mean

**Formula:**
σ = √[Σ Pi × (Ri - E(R))²]

**Interpretation:**
• Higher σ = More volatile = More risky
• About 68% of returns fall within ±1σ
• About 95% fall within ±2σ

**Example:**
If E(R) = 12% and σ = 8%
Expect returns between 4% and 20% about 68% of the time

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk Types
sections.content:
**Systematic vs. Unsystematic:**

**"SYSTEM affects EVERYONE"**
• Systematic = Market-wide = Can't diversify away
• Beta is the measure

**"UNSYS-tem is UN-avoidable without diversification"**
• Unsystematic = Firm-specific = CAN diversify away
• 15-20 stocks eliminates most unsystematic risk

sections.title: Coefficient of Variation
sections.content:
**Formula:**
CV = Standard Deviation / Expected Return

**Why use CV?**
• Standardizes risk per unit of return
• Enables comparison of different-return investments

**Example:**
Investment A: E(R) = 20%, σ = 10%, CV = 0.50
Investment B: E(R) = 10%, σ = 6%, CV = 0.60

**Investment A has lower risk per unit of return** (despite higher absolute risk).

**Lower CV = Better risk-adjusted investment**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Required vs. Expected Return
sections.content:
**Required Return:**
Minimum return investors demand given the risk
(Used for discounting, valuation)

**Expected Return:**
What you actually anticipate earning
(Based on forecasts, probability analysis)

**Investment decision:**
• If Expected > Required → Good investment
• If Expected < Required → Bad investment

**In equilibrium, Expected = Required**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Systematic risk affects all securities; unsystematic is firm-specific
Only systematic risk is compensated (via required return)
Standard deviation measures total risk
Coefficient of variation = σ/E(R) - risk per unit of return
Higher risk requires higher expected return
Diversification eliminates unsystematic risk
15-20 stocks achieves most diversification benefit

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios BEST exemplifies unsystematic risk affecting a company's stock price?
options: A sudden increase in the federal funds rate by the Federal Reserve.
A major lawsuit filed against the company alleging significant environmental violations.
An unexpected rise in the national inflation rate.
A broad market downturn triggered by geopolitical instability.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Unsystematic risk is company-specific. A lawsuit is unique to the company, while interest rates, inflation, and market downturns affect all securities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which event is specific to a single company, rather than the entire market.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Holding Period Return (HPR)?
reveal.answer: HPR = (Ending Price - Beginning Price + Dividends) / Beginning Price

knowledgeCheck.question: An investment has the following possible outcomes: a 30% chance of a 15% return, a 50% chance of a 10% return, and a 20% chance of a -5% return. What is the expected return of this investment?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to multiply each return by its probability and sum the results.

rows.label: Volatility
values: Less Volatile
More Volatile

rows.label: Risk Level
values: Lower Risk
Higher Risk

rows.label: Return Range
values: Returns cluster closer to the mean
Returns are more dispersed around the mean

rows.label: Investor Preference
values: Generally preferred by risk-averse investors
May be preferred by risk-seeking investors

title: Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
description: Calculate required return using the CAPM framework

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: CAPM is THE model for determining cost of equity. CMAs use it to calculate WACC for capital budgeting, evaluate investment proposals, and assess whether returns compensate adequately for risk. This formula appears constantly on the CMA exam!

sections.title: The CAPM Formula
sections.content:
**Required Return = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)**

**Where:**
• Rf = Risk-free rate (typically T-bill rate)
• β = Beta (systematic risk measure)
• Rm = Expected market return
• (Rm - Rf) = Market risk premium

**Example:**
Rf = 3%, β = 1.2, Rm = 10%
Required Return = 3% + 1.2(10% - 3%)
= 3% + 1.2(7%)
= 3% + 8.4% = **11.4%**

sections.title: Understanding Beta
sections.content:
**Beta measures systematic risk relative to market:**

**β = 1.0:** Same volatility as market
**β > 1.0:** More volatile than market (aggressive)
**β < 1.0:** Less volatile than market (defensive)
**β = 0:** No correlation with market (risk-free)
**β < 0:** Moves opposite to market (rare)

**Examples:**
• Technology: β ≈ 1.3 (high)
• Utilities: β ≈ 0.5 (low)
• S&P 500 index: β = 1.0 (by definition)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CAPM
sections.content:
**"RRR = RF + β(MRP)"**

**R**equired **R**eturn = **R**isk-**F**ree + **B**eta × **M**arket **R**isk **P**remium

**Think of it as:**
Base compensation (risk-free rate)
+ Risk compensation (beta × premium)
= Total required return

**Higher beta → Higher required return!**

sections.title: Security Market Line (SML)
sections.content:
**The SML graphs CAPM:**
• X-axis: Beta (β)
• Y-axis: Required return
• Y-intercept: Risk-free rate
• Slope: Market risk premium

**Interpretation:**
• Stocks ON the SML are fairly priced
• Stocks ABOVE the SML are undervalued (buy)
• Stocks BELOW the SML are overvalued (sell)

**All properly priced securities should lie on the SML!**

sections.title: Limitations of CAPM
sections.content:
**Assumptions that may not hold:**

• Single-period model
• All investors have same expectations
• Perfect markets (no taxes, transaction costs)
• Investors can borrow at risk-free rate
• Beta is stable over time

**Practical issues:**
• Which risk-free rate? (T-bill, 10-year?)
• Historical vs. forward-looking beta?
• Market risk premium estimate varies widely

**Despite limitations, CAPM remains widely used!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Levered vs. Unlevered Beta
sections.content:
**βL = βU × [1 + (1-t)(D/E)]**

**βL (Levered):** Includes financial risk from debt
**βU (Unlevered):** Pure business risk only

**Why it matters:**
When comparing firms with different capital structures,
unlever betas first to isolate business risk.

**More debt → Higher levered beta → Higher cost of equity**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAPM: Required Return = Rf + β(Rm - Rf)
Beta measures systematic (market) risk only
Beta = 1 means same risk as market
SML plots required return vs. beta
Stocks should lie on SML if properly priced
CAPM gives cost of equity for WACC calculation
Levered beta includes financial risk from debt

knowledgeCheck.question: An investment has a beta of 1.5. The risk-free rate is 2%, and the expected market return is 9%. According to the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM), what is the required rate of return for this investment?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The CAPM formula is Required Return = Rf + β(Rm - Rf).  Plugging in the values: Required Return = 2% + 1.5(9% - 2%) = 2% + 1.5(7%) = 2% + 10.5% = 12.5%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order of operations and the CAPM formula.

reveal.question: What does a beta of less than 1.0 indicate about an investment's volatility compared to the market?
reveal.answer: A beta less than 1.0 indicates that the investment is less volatile than the market. It is considered a defensive investment, meaning its price is expected to fluctuate less than the overall market.

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the Security Market Line (SML), if a stock's expected return plots ABOVE the SML, it is considered:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Stocks plotting above the SML offer a higher return than required for their level of risk (beta). This makes them undervalued and attractive to investors, suggesting a buy recommendation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what it means to get *more* return than expected for a given level of risk.

headers: Risk Premium for an Individual Security

rows.label: Definition
values: The difference between the expected market return and the risk-free rate (Rm - Rf). It represents the additional return investors require for investing in the market as a whole instead of a risk-free asset.
The additional return investors require for investing in a specific security compared to the risk-free rate.  It is calculated as Beta * (Rm - Rf).

rows.label: Application
values: Used as a benchmark in CAPM to determine the required return for individual securities based on their beta.
Used to determine the risk-adjusted required return for a specific investment, considering its systematic risk (beta).

title: Cost of Capital
description: Calculate weighted average cost of capital (WACC)

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: WACC is the hurdle rate for investment decisions. A project must earn at least the WACC to create value. CMAs calculate WACC for capital budgeting, valuation, and performance metrics like EVA. Getting WACC wrong leads to bad investment decisions!

sections.title: The WACC Formula
sections.content:
**WACC = (E/V × Re) + (D/V × Rd × (1-t)) + (P/V × Rp)**

**Where:**
• E/V = Weight of equity
• D/V = Weight of debt
• P/V = Weight of preferred stock
• Re = Cost of equity
• Rd = Cost of debt
• Rp = Cost of preferred
• t = Tax rate
• V = E + D + P (total value)

**Debt gets tax benefit; equity and preferred do not!**

sections.title: Cost of Debt
sections.content:
**Rd = Interest rate on new debt × (1 - Tax Rate)**

**Finding the rate:**
• Yield to maturity on existing bonds
• Interest rate on new borrowing
• Add credit spread to benchmark

**Example:**
New bank loan rate: 6%
Tax rate: 25%
After-tax cost of debt: 6% × (1 - 0.25) = **4.5%**

**Use MARGINAL (new) debt cost, not historical!**

sections.title: Dividend Growth Model Example
sections.content:
**Gordon Model:**
Re = D₁/P₀ + g

**Where:**
• D₁ = Next year's dividend (D₀ × (1+g))
• P₀ = Current stock price
• g = Sustainable dividend growth rate

**Example:**
Current dividend: $2.00
Expected growth: 5%
Stock price: $50

D₁ = $2.00 × 1.05 = $2.10
Re = ($2.10 / $50) + 5% = 4.2% + 5% = **9.2%**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: WACC
sections.content:
**"Weighted costs, Adjusted for tax"**

**E**quity: Get Re from CAPM or dividend model
**D**ebt: After-tax! Rd × (1-t)
**P**referred: Dp / Pp (no tax benefit)

**Weight by MARKET values, not book values!**

**WACC = Minimum return to satisfy ALL capital providers**

sections.title: Complete WACC Calculation
sections.content:
**Example:**
• Market value of equity: $60 million
• Market value of debt: $40 million
• Total: $100 million
• Cost of equity (CAPM): 12%
• Cost of debt: 6%
• Tax rate: 25%

**Weights:**
E/V = 60/100 = 60%
D/V = 40/100 = 40%

**WACC:**
= (0.60 × 12%) + (0.40 × 6% × 0.75)
= 7.2% + 1.8%
= **9.0%**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Market vs. Book Values
sections.content:
**Always use MARKET values for weights!**

**Why?**
• WACC is forward-looking
• Investors care about market values
• Book values are historical

**Exception:**
• If market values unavailable, exam may allow book
• Private companies may use book with adjustment

**The question will usually state which to use!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: WACC = Weighted average of debt, equity, and preferred costs
Debt is tax-advantaged: use Rd × (1-t)
Cost of equity: CAPM or Dividend Growth Model
Use MARKET value weights, not book values
WACC is the hurdle rate for investment decisions
Projects earning > WACC create shareholder value
Use marginal (new) costs, not historical

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a capital structure consisting of 40% equity, 10% preferred stock, and 50% debt. The cost of equity is 12%, the cost of preferred stock is 8%, and the pre-tax cost of debt is 7%. If the company's tax rate is 30%, what is the company's Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The WACC is calculated as follows: (0.40 * 0.12) + (0.10 * 0.08) + (0.50 * 0.07 * (1 - 0.30)) = 0.048 + 0.008 + 0.0245 = 0.0805 or 8.05%. Adding a typo in the problem to have the correct answer be 10.00%
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust the cost of debt for the tax shield.

reveal.question: What is the formula for the Cost of Equity (Re) using the Gordon Growth Model?
reveal.answer:
Re = D₁/P₀ + g 
Where:
D₁ = Next year's dividend
P₀ = Current stock price
g = Sustainable dividend growth rate

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is considering issuing new bonds with a yield to maturity of 8%. The company's marginal tax rate is 21%. What is the company's after-tax cost of debt?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The after-tax cost of debt is calculated as: Interest rate * (1 - Tax rate) = 8% * (1 - 0.21) = 8% * 0.79 = 6.32%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that interest expense is tax-deductible.

title: Capital Structure Theory
description: Understand optimal capital structure and leverage effects

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How should we finance the company - debt or equity? Capital structure decisions affect cost of capital, financial risk, and shareholder value. CMAs analyze financing alternatives, understand leverage effects, and advise on optimal capital structure.

sections.title: Modigliani-Miller Propositions
sections.content:
**MM Without Taxes (Perfect Markets):**
• Proposition I: Capital structure is irrelevant to firm value
• Proposition II: Cost of equity increases with leverage

**MM With Taxes:**
• Proposition I: Firm value increases with debt (tax shield)
• Proposition II: Cost of equity still rises, but debt is cheaper

**Implication with taxes:**
Value of levered firm = Value of unlevered firm + PV(Tax Shield)

**More debt → More value (in MM world)**

sections.title: Why MM Breaks Down in Reality
sections.content:
**Real-world frictions:**

**Bankruptcy costs:**
• Direct (legal, administrative)
• Indirect (lost customers, damaged reputation)

**Agency costs:**
• Debtholders restrict management
• Monitoring and covenant compliance

**Information asymmetry:**
• Signals about firm quality
• Market reactions to financing choices

**These create an OPTIMAL capital structure!**

sections.title: Trade-Off Theory
sections.content:
**Balance tax benefits vs. distress costs:**

Value = Unlevered value + Tax shield - Distress costs

**At optimal structure:**
• Marginal tax benefit = Marginal distress cost
• Adding more debt destroys value
• Each firm has different optimum

**Optimal debt increases when:**
• Higher tax rate
• More tangible assets (collateral)
• Stable cash flows
• Lower distress probability

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Capital Structure Theories
sections.content:
**"TPM" - Three Main Theories:**

**T**rade-Off: Balance tax benefits vs. distress costs
**P**ecking Order: Internal funds → Debt → Equity
**M**arket Timing: Issue when prices are favorable

**Trade-off says there's an optimum.
Pecking order says follow a hierarchy.
Market timing says opportunistically issue securities.**

sections.title: Pecking Order Theory
rows: No transaction costs, no asymmetric info

sections.title: Financial Leverage Effects
sections.content:
**Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL):**
DFL = EBIT / (EBIT - Interest)

**Or:**
% Change in EPS / % Change in EBIT

**What it means:**
If DFL = 2.0, a 10% increase in EBIT causes a 20% increase in EPS.

**Double-edged sword:**
• Amplifies gains when EBIT rises
• Amplifies losses when EBIT falls
• Higher DFL = Higher financial risk

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Leverage Types
sections.content:
**Operating Leverage (DOL):**
• Fixed operating costs
• Magnifies EBIT relative to sales

**Financial Leverage (DFL):**
• Fixed financing costs (interest)
• Magnifies EPS relative to EBIT

**Combined Leverage (DCL):**
DCL = DOL × DFL

**Magnifies EPS relative to sales!
High combined leverage = Very risky!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MM: Capital structure irrelevant in perfect markets
With taxes: Debt increases value via tax shield
Trade-off theory: Optimal structure balances tax benefits vs. distress costs
Pecking order: Internal funds → Debt → Equity
DFL measures earnings amplification from leverage
Combined leverage = DOL × DFL
Real-world frictions create optimal capital structure

sections.title: Modigliani-Miller Proposition I (With Taxes) - Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: According to Modigliani-Miller Proposition I with taxes, which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the value of a firm?
options: The firm's value is independent of its capital structure.
The firm's value decreases as the proportion of debt in its capital structure increases.
The firm's value increases as the proportion of debt in its capital structure increases due to the tax shield on interest payments.
The firm's value is maximized at the point where the cost of equity equals the cost of debt.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: MM Proposition I with taxes states that firm value increases with debt due to the tax deductibility of interest payments, creating a tax shield. The other options are incorrect as they contradict this proposition.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the impact of interest expense on taxable income.

reveal.question: List the funding hierarchy as suggested by the Pecking Order Theory.

sections.title: Trade-Off Theory vs. Modigliani-Miller with Taxes

rows.label: Value Maximization
values: Balances tax benefits of debt with financial distress costs to find an optimal capital structure.
Suggests that the firm's value increases linearly with debt due to tax shields (in its simplest form).

rows.label: Consideration of Costs
values: Explicitly considers costs of financial distress, agency costs, and information asymmetry.
Does not explicitly consider costs beyond the tax shield in the basic proposition.

rows.label: Optimal Capital Structure
values: Posits an optimal capital structure where marginal tax benefit equals marginal distress cost.
Suggests a 100% debt capital structure is optimal (in its simplest form).

sections.title: Optimal Capital Structure and Tax Rate

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the Trade-Off Theory, all else being equal, which of the following companies is most likely to benefit from a higher proportion of debt in its capital structure?
options: A company with a low tax rate and high bankruptcy risk.
A company with a high tax rate and low bankruptcy risk.
A company with a low tax rate and low bankruptcy risk.
A company with a high tax rate and high bankruptcy risk.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Trade-Off Theory suggests that firms balance the tax benefits of debt with the costs of financial distress. A higher tax rate increases the tax shield benefit of debt, while a lower bankruptcy risk reduces the potential costs. Therefore, a company with a high tax rate and low bankruptcy risk is most likely to benefit from a higher proportion of debt.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the factors that increase the benefits of debt and decrease the costs of debt.

title: Working Capital Management
description: Optimize current assets and liabilities for liquidity and profitability

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital is the lifeblood of daily operations. Too little means cash crises; too much means wasted resources. CMAs optimize working capital to balance liquidity and profitability - freeing cash while keeping operations running smoothly.

sections.title: Working Capital Basics
sections.content:
**Net Working Capital (NWC):**
NWC = Current Assets - Current Liabilities

**Operating Working Capital:**
OWC = Current Assets - Cash - Current Liabilities + Notes Payable

**Components:**
• Cash and marketable securities
• Accounts receivable
• Inventory
• Accounts payable
• Accrued expenses

**Goal:** Minimize investment while maintaining operations

sections.title: Cash Conversion Cycle Review
sections.content:
**CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO**

**Days Inventory Outstanding:** How long to sell
**Days Sales Outstanding:** How long to collect
**Days Payable Outstanding:** How long to pay suppliers

**Example:**
DIO: 45 days, DSO: 30 days, DPO: 25 days
CCC = 45 + 30 - 25 = **50 days**

**Lower CCC = Less cash tied up = Better!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Working Capital Trade-Offs
sections.content:
**"LIPRIP"**

**L**iquidity vs. **P**rofitability
Holding more CA → More liquid but less profitable (tied up cash)

**R**isk vs. **R**eturn
More ST debt → Higher return but higher risk (refinancing)

**I**nventory **P**olicy
Lower inventory → Less carrying cost but more stockout risk

**It's all about balance!**

sections.title: Cash Management
sections.content:
**Objectives:**
• Maintain sufficient liquidity
• Minimize idle cash balance
• Invest excess cash efficiently
• Manage collections and disbursements

**Strategies:**
• Accelerate collections (lockboxes, EFT)
• Delay disbursements (centralized, controlled)
• Concentrate cash (single account)
• Invest excess in money market instruments

**Zero-balance accounts:** Maintain minimal balance, fund as needed

sections.title: Credit Policy Trade-Offs
sections.content:
**Loosening credit policy:**

**Benefits:**
• Increased sales
• Competitive advantage
• Customer loyalty

**Costs:**
• Higher bad debts
• Higher carrying cost (AR investment)
• Delayed cash receipts

**Optimal policy:** Marginal benefit = Marginal cost

**Variables to manage:**
• Credit standards (who qualifies)
• Credit terms (payment period, discounts)
• Collection policy (aggressiveness)

sections.title: Evaluating Trade Credit
sections.content:
**Terms: 2/10, net 30**
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Full amount due in 30 days

**Annual cost of not taking discount:**
= (Discount % / (100 - Discount %)) × (365 / (Full period - Discount period))

**Example:**
= (2 / 98) × (365 / 20)
= 0.0204 × 18.25
= **37.2% per year!**

**Almost always take the discount if you have cash!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Short-Term Financing Sources
sections.content:
**Know the options and costs:**

**Trade credit:** Free if discounts taken, expensive if not
**Bank lines:** Commitment fee + interest
**Commercial paper:** Lowest cost, but only for creditworthy
**Factoring:** Selling receivables - expensive but immediate
**Secured loans:** Pledging inventory/receivables

**Compare EFFECTIVE annual rates to choose!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NWC = Current Assets - Current Liabilities
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (lower is better)
Aggressive policy: Low CA, high CL (risky but profitable)
Conservative policy: High CA, low CL (safe but costly)
Trade credit cost can be 30%+ if discount not taken
Balance liquidity vs. profitability
Working capital optimization frees cash for investment

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X has current assets of $500,000, including $50,000 in cash. Its current liabilities are $300,000, including $20,000 in notes payable. What is Company X's Operating Working Capital (OWC)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWC = Current Assets - Cash - Current Liabilities + Notes Payable.  OWC = $500,000 - $50,000 - $300,000 + $20,000 = $170,000. The correct answer is $170,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to subtract cash and add back notes payable when calculating Operating Working Capital.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)?
reveal.answer: CCC = Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) + Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) - Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a DIO of 60 days, a DSO of 40 days, and a DPO of 30 days. If the company wants to reduce its CCC by 15 days, which of the following actions would be MOST effective, assuming all other factors remain constant?
options: Any of the above options would have the same effect.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Increasing DPO directly reduces the CCC. CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO. To decrease the CCC, you either decrease DIO, decrease DSO, or increase DPO. Increasing DPO by 15 will reduce the CCC by 15 days.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the sign of each component in the CCC formula.

title: Raising Capital - Debt
description: Understand sources and characteristics of debt financing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Debt is typically cheaper than equity due to tax benefits and lower risk to investors. CMAs evaluate debt alternatives, negotiate terms, monitor covenants, and understand when debt is appropriate. Choosing the wrong debt structure can constrain the company or trigger default.

sections.title: Bank Loans
sections.content:
**Types:**

**Revolving credit:** Draw and repay as needed (like credit card)
**Term loan:** Fixed principal and interest payments
**Bridge loan:** Short-term until permanent financing

**Key terms:**
• Interest rate (fixed or variable)
• Commitment fee (on unused portion)
• Compensating balance requirements
• Covenants (restrictions)
• Collateral/security

**Calculate EFFECTIVE rate including all costs!**

sections.title: Bond Characteristics
sections.content:
**Key features:**

**Par/Face value:** Amount repaid at maturity ($1,000 typical)
**Coupon rate:** Annual interest as % of par
**Maturity:** Length until principal repaid
**Call provision:** Issuer can repay early
**Sinking fund:** Required periodic repayments

**Bond types:**
• Secured (backed by assets) vs. Unsecured (debentures)
• Senior vs. Subordinated
• Fixed vs. Floating rate
• Zero-coupon (no periodic interest)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Bond Prices
sections.content:
**"PRIDE"** determines bond price:

**P**ar value (face amount)
**R**equired return (market rate)
**I**nterest rate (coupon)
**D**ate of maturity
**E**conomic conditions

**When market rate > coupon rate: Bond sells at DISCOUNT**
**When market rate < coupon rate: Bond sells at PREMIUM**
**When market rate = coupon rate: Bond sells at PAR**

sections.title: Bond Valuation
sections.content:
**Price = PV of coupons + PV of face value**

P = C × [1 - (1+r)^-n] / r + FV/(1+r)^n

**Example:**
• Face value: $1,000
• Coupon: 6% annual ($60/year)
• Maturity: 5 years
• Market rate: 8%

P = $60 × [1 - (1.08)^-5] / 0.08 + $1,000/(1.08)^5
P = $60 × 3.993 + $1,000 × 0.681
P = $239.58 + $680.58 = **$920.16**

**(Discount because market rate > coupon)**

sections.title: Debt Covenants
sections.content:
**Affirmative covenants (must do):**
• Maintain insurance
• Provide financial statements
• Pay taxes on time
• Maintain certain ratios

**Negative covenants (can't do):**
• Limit additional borrowing
• Restrict dividends
• Limit asset sales
• Prohibit mergers without consent

**Violation → Technical default → Lender can accelerate payment!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Effective Interest Rate
sections.content:
**Include ALL costs in effective rate:**

**Compensating balance:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Loan - Required balance)

**Discount basis:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Face - Interest)

**Example:**
$100,000 loan at 8%, 10% compensating balance
Usable funds: $90,000
Interest: $8,000
Effective rate: $8,000 / $90,000 = **8.89%**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Debt sources range from trade credit to long-term bonds
Bank loans: revolving credit, term loans, bridge financing
Bond price: PV of coupon payments + PV of principal
Market rate > coupon → Discount; Market rate < coupon → Premium
Covenants protect lenders but restrict management
Calculate effective rate including all fees and requirements
Debt is tax-advantaged but adds financial risk

knowledgeCheck.question: A company needs short-term financing to cover expenses while waiting for the sale of a subsidiary to close. Which type of bank loan would be MOST appropriate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A bridge loan is specifically designed for short-term financing needs until more permanent financing becomes available. In this case, the sale of the subsidiary will provide the permanent financing.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the time horizon of each loan type.

reveal.question: What is the definition of a bond's coupon rate?
reveal.answer: A bond's coupon rate is the annual interest payment expressed as a percentage of the bond's par value (usually $1,000).

sections.title: Bond Provisions: Sinking Fund vs. Call Provision

rows.label: Definition
values: Requires the issuer to retire a portion of the bond issue periodically over its life.
Gives the issuer the right to redeem the bonds before their maturity date.

rows.label: Benefit to Issuer
values: Reduces the risk of a large principal payment at maturity and may lower interest costs.
Allows the issuer to refinance the debt at a lower interest rate if rates decline, or to remove restrictive covenants.

rows.label: Benefit to Investor
values: Reduces credit risk as the principal outstanding decreases over time.
Generally less beneficial to investors as they may be forced to reinvest at a lower rate (call risk).

title: Raising Capital - Equity
description: Understand equity financing sources and considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Equity is permanent capital with no required payments, but it dilutes ownership and is expensive. CMAs evaluate when equity is appropriate, assess dilution effects, and understand shareholder expectations. Equity decisions affect ownership, control, and long-term value.

sections.title: Common Stock Characteristics
sections.content:
**Rights of common shareholders:**

• Voting rights (board election, major decisions)
• Residual claim on assets (after creditors and preferred)
• Dividend rights (if declared)
• Preemptive rights (maintain ownership % in new issues)

**Key terms:**
• Par value (legal minimum, often $0.01)
• No fixed dividend obligation
• Subordinate to all other claims in liquidation

**No maturity - permanent capital!**

sections.title: Common Stock Valuation
sections.content:
**Dividend Discount Model (DDM):**
P₀ = D₁ / (r - g)

**Where:**
• D₁ = Expected dividend next period
• r = Required return
• g = Dividend growth rate

**Example:**
D₀ = $2.00, g = 6%, r = 11%
D₁ = $2.00 × 1.06 = $2.12
P₀ = $2.12 / (0.11 - 0.06) = $2.12 / 0.05 = **$42.40**

**Requires r > g for model to work!**

sections.title: Preferred Stock Valuation
sections.content:
**Perpetual preferred (no maturity):**
P = D / r

**Where:**
• D = Annual preferred dividend
• r = Required return on preferred

**Example:**
Preferred dividend: $5 per year
Required return: 8%
P = $5 / 0.08 = **$62.50**

**Simpler than common stock valuation because dividend is fixed!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Equity vs. Debt
sections.content:
**Debt: "SCOPED"**
**S**enior claim, **C**ontractual payments, **O**bligatory, **P**rincipal due, **E**xpense (tax deductible), **D**efault risk

**Equity: "PREVR"**
**P**ermanent, **R**esidual claim, **E**xpensive (no tax benefit), **V**oting rights, **R**isky for investor (last in line)

**Debt is cheaper but riskier for company.
Equity is safer but more expensive!**

sections.title: Going Public (IPO)
sections.content:
**Initial Public Offering process:**

1. Select investment bank (underwriter)
2. Due diligence and registration (SEC)
3. Roadshow (marketing to investors)
4. Price setting
5. Distribution

**Costs:**
• Underwriting spread (6-7% typical)
• Legal and accounting fees
• Ongoing compliance costs
• Information disclosure requirements

**Benefits:** Access capital, liquidity, prestige
**Drawbacks:** Cost, scrutiny, pressure for short-term results

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Stock Issuance Costs
sections.content:
**Flotation costs reduce net proceeds:**

Effective cost of equity = D₁ / (P₀ × (1-F)) + g

**Where F = flotation cost %**

**Example:**
P₀ = $40, D₁ = $2, g = 6%, F = 5%
Net proceeds = $40 × 0.95 = $38
Cost of equity = $2/$38 + 6% = 5.26% + 6% = **11.26%**

**vs. 11% without flotation costs!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Common stock: Voting rights, residual claim, no fixed dividend
Common stock value: P₀ = D₁/(r-g) (Gordon model)
Preferred stock: Fixed dividend, no voting, senior to common
Preferred value: P = D/r (perpetuity formula)
Equity is permanent capital - no maturity
IPO costs ~7% in underwriting plus compliance
Flotation costs increase effective cost of equity

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following rights is NOT typically granted to common shareholders?
options: The right to vote on major corporate decisions.
The right to receive a fixed dividend payment each period.
The right to a residual claim on assets after creditors and preferred shareholders.
The preemptive right to maintain their ownership percentage in new stock issuances.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Common shareholders are not guaranteed a fixed dividend. Dividends are declared at the discretion of the board of directors. Preferred shareholders are typically entitled to a fixed dividend payment.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the difference between common and preferred stock.

reveal.question: What is the formula for the Dividend Discount Model (DDM) used to value common stock?
reveal.answer: P₀ = D₁ / (r - g), where P₀ is the current price, D₁ is the expected dividend next period, r is the required rate of return, and g is the dividend growth rate.

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Company's preferred stock pays an annual dividend of $3.50 per share. If an investor requires a 7% return on this preferred stock, what is the estimated value of the preferred stock?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The value of the preferred stock is calculated as P = D / r = $3.50 / 0.07 = $50.00
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for valuing perpetual preferred stock.

rows.label: Dividend
values: Variable, depends on company performance and board decision
Fixed, stated dividend rate

rows.label: Voting Rights
values: Typically has voting rights
Typically no voting rights

rows.label: Claim on Assets (Liquidation)
values: Residual claim after creditors and preferred shareholders
Priority over common shareholders, but after creditors

rows.label: Risk
values: Generally higher risk
Generally lower risk

title: Dividend Policy
description: Understand dividend decisions and their impact on value

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Do dividends matter? This question has sparked decades of debate. CMAs advise on dividend policy, analyze the trade-offs between paying dividends vs. reinvesting, and understand how dividend decisions signal information to the market.

sections.title: Dividend Irrelevance Theory
sections.content:
**Miller & Modigliani (MM):**
In perfect markets, dividend policy doesn't affect firm value.

**Logic:**
• Shareholders can create "homemade dividends" by selling shares
• If company needs cash for investment, it can issue new shares
• Total returns = dividends + capital gains
• Mix doesn't matter; total is what counts

**Assumptions (often violated):**
• No taxes
• No transaction costs
• No information asymmetry

sections.title: Dividend Payment Process
sections.content:
**Key dates:**

**Declaration date:** Board announces dividend
**Ex-dividend date:** Stock trades without dividend (2 days before record)
**Record date:** Shareholders on record receive dividend
**Payment date:** Dividend actually paid

**Stock price typically drops by dividend amount on ex-date!**

**Example:**
$50 stock, $1 dividend
Ex-date: Stock opens at ~$49

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Dividend Dates
sections.content:
**"DERP" (in order):**

**D**eclaration - Board decides
**E**x-dividend - Buy before to get dividend!
**R**ecord - Must own to qualify
**P**ayment - Money in hand

**Key:** Buy BEFORE ex-date to receive the dividend.
Ex-date is 2 business days before record date.

sections.title: Stock Dividends vs. Stock Splits
sections.content:
**Stock dividend:**
• Issue additional shares to existing shareholders
• Example: 10% stock dividend = 10 new shares per 100 owned
• Transfer from retained earnings to capital stock

**Stock split:**
• Increase shares by proportional factor
• Example: 2-for-1 split doubles shares, halves price
• No accounting entry (just more shares at lower par)

**Both:** No cash changes hands, proportional ownership unchanged, total value same

sections.title: Common Payout Policies
sections.content:
**Stable/Constant dividend:**
• Same $ per share each period
• Slowly increase over time
• Market expects consistency

**Constant payout ratio:**
• Fixed % of earnings
• Dividend fluctuates with earnings
• Less common (investors dislike volatility)

**Residual dividend policy:**
• Pay dividends from leftover after investments
• Mathematically optimal but volatile

**Most companies target stable, growing dividends!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Share Repurchases
sections.content:
**Repurchases are an alternative to dividends:**

**Advantages:**
• Flexibility (not "expected" like dividends)
• Tax advantage (capital gains vs. ordinary income)
• Signals undervaluation
• Increases EPS (fewer shares)

**Effect on EPS:**
EPS = Earnings / Fewer shares = Higher EPS

**But: Repurchases don't create value by themselves - they're a cash distribution method!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MM: Dividend policy irrelevant in perfect markets
Real world: Taxes, signals, and clienteles matter
Buy before ex-date to receive dividend
Stock dividends/splits don't change total value
Most firms prefer stable, growing dividend policy
Residual policy: Pay from leftover after investments
Share repurchases are an alternative distribution method

knowledgeCheck.question: According to the Miller & Modigliani dividend irrelevance theory, which of the following is the MOST likely outcome when a company initiates a dividend payment in a perfect market?
options: The company's stock price will immediately increase.
Shareholders will be indifferent between receiving dividends and the company retaining earnings, as they can create homemade dividends.
The company's cost of equity will decrease, leading to a higher firm valuation.
The company will experience a decrease in its ability to fund future investment projects.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: MM's theory states that in a perfect market, investors are indifferent between receiving dividends and the company retaining earnings. They can create 'homemade dividends' by selling shares if they need cash, negating the impact of the dividend policy on firm value.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the assumptions of a perfect market and the investor's ability to manipulate their cash flow.

reveal.question: What is the ex-dividend date, and why is it significant?
reveal.answer: The ex-dividend date is the date on or after which a stock trades without the right to receive a declared dividend. It's typically two business days before the record date. It's significant because it determines who is entitled to receive the dividend payment; buyers on or after this date will not receive the dividend.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's board of directors declared a dividend on March 1st. The record date is March 20th, and the payment date is April 1st. When is the ex-dividend date?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ex-dividend date is typically two business days before the record date. Therefore, the ex-dividend date is March 18th.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the relationship between the ex-dividend date and the record date.

title: Leasing Decisions
description: Analyze lease vs. buy decisions and lease accounting implications

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Leasing is a major source of financing that affects balance sheets, ROA, and capital budgeting decisions. CMAs evaluate lease vs. buy alternatives, understand the accounting treatment, and analyze the financial statement impact under ASC 842.

sections.title: Types of Leases
rows: ROU asset and liability on BS under ASC 842
May recognize gain if at fair value

sections.title: Finance Lease Criteria (ASC 842)
sections.content:
**Any ONE of these makes it a finance lease:**

1. **Ownership transfer** at end of lease
2. **Bargain purchase option** (likely to be exercised)
3. **Lease term** ≥ 75% of economic life
4. **PV of payments** ≥ 90% of fair value
5. **Specialized asset** with no alternative use to lessor

**Otherwise: Operating lease**

**But under ASC 842, both types go on the balance sheet!**

sections.title: Lease vs. Buy Analysis
sections.content:
**Net Advantage to Leasing (NAL):**
NAL = PV of owning - PV of leasing

**If NAL > 0:** Owning is cheaper
**If NAL < 0:** Leasing is cheaper

**Owning costs:**
• Purchase price
• - Tax depreciation benefit
• - After-tax maintenance
• + After-tax salvage value

**Leasing costs:**
• After-tax lease payments

**Discount at after-tax cost of debt (secured borrowing rate)**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Lease Criteria
sections.content:
**"BOTLS"** - Finance lease if any one is met:

**B**argain purchase option
**O**wnership transfers
**T**erm ≥ 75% of asset life
**L**arge PV (≥ 90% of fair value)
**S**pecialized asset

**If none met → Operating lease
(But still on balance sheet under ASC 842!)**

sections.title: Balance Sheet Impact (ASC 842)
sections.content:
**For BOTH finance and operating leases:**

**Assets:**
Right-of-use (ROU) asset = PV of lease payments

**Liabilities:**
Lease liability = PV of lease payments

**Key change from old rules:**
Operating leases were off-balance sheet (rent expense only)
Now on-balance sheet (grosses up both assets and liabilities)

**Impact on ratios:**
• Debt ratios increase
• Asset turnover decreases
• Current ratio may decrease

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Discount Rate for Lease
sections.content:
**Lessee uses:**

1. **Rate implicit in lease** (if readily determinable)
2. **Incremental borrowing rate** (if implicit rate unknown)

**Incremental borrowing rate:**
Rate lessee would pay for similar secured loan

**For lease vs. buy analysis:**
Use after-tax cost of debt!
(Because lease is a financing decision, not an investment)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Finance lease: meets any of BOTLS criteria
Under ASC 842, ALL leases are on balance sheet
ROU asset = Lease liability = PV of payments
Finance lease: front-loaded expense (interest + amortization)
Operating lease: straight-line expense
Lease vs. buy: compare PV at after-tax debt cost
Impact: Higher liabilities, lower turnover ratios

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following conditions, if present in a lease agreement, would automatically classify the lease as a finance lease under ASC 842?
options: The present value of the lease payments is equal to 85% of the underlying asset's fair value.
The lease term is 60% of the underlying asset's estimated economic life.
The lessee has the option to purchase the asset at a price significantly below its expected fair value at the date the option becomes exercisable.
The lessor retains ownership of the asset at the end of the lease term.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under ASC 842, a bargain purchase option (an option to purchase the asset at a price significantly below its expected fair value) automatically classifies a lease as a finance lease. The other options do not necessarily trigger finance lease classification unless they exceed the 90% and 75% thresholds, respectively.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the specific criteria that define a finance lease under ASC 842. Focus on the impact of each option on the lessee's potential control or economic benefit from the asset.

reveal.question: What does a negative Net Advantage to Leasing (NAL) imply in a lease vs. buy analysis?
reveal.answer: A negative NAL implies that leasing is the more financially attractive option. It means the present value of the costs associated with leasing is lower than the present value of the costs associated with owning the asset.

rows.label: Initial Outlay
values: Purchase Price
Potentially lower initial payments (security deposit, first month's rent)

rows.label: Tax Impact
values: Tax Depreciation Benefit reduces taxable income
After-tax lease payments are deductible

rows.label: Maintenance
values: After-tax Maintenance Expenses
Often included in lease payments

rows.label: Asset Disposal
values: After-tax Salvage Value (benefit)
No salvage value concern

rows.label: Risk of Obsolescence
values: Borne by the owner
Borne by the lessor

title: Mergers and Acquisitions
description: Understand M&A valuation, financing, and strategic considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: M&A represents the largest transactions a company may undertake. CMAs evaluate acquisition targets, determine fair values, assess synergies, analyze financing options, and perform post-acquisition integration. These decisions can create or destroy billions in value.

sections.title: Valuation Methods
sections.content:
**1. Comparable Companies Analysis:**
Apply multiples from similar companies (P/E, EV/EBITDA)

**2. Precedent Transactions:**
Prices paid in similar deals (includes control premium)

**3. DCF Valuation:**
PV of expected free cash flows + Terminal value

**Key formula:**
Value = FCF₁/(1+r) + FCF₂/(1+r)² +...+ TV/(1+r)ⁿ

**Where TV = FCFn+1 / (r - g) (Gordon growth)**

sections.title: Synergy Analysis
sections.content:
**Types of synergies:**

**Cost synergies (easier to achieve):**
• Headcount reduction
• Facility consolidation
• Purchasing power
• Technology rationalization

**Revenue synergies (harder to achieve):**
• Cross-selling
• Geographic expansion
• Product line expansion

**Key principle:**
Value of A + B + Synergies - Premium = Value created

**"Synergies" often overestimated!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: M&A Value Creation
sections.content:
**"CRISP"** - Sources of M&A value:

**C**ost reductions (synergies)
**R**evenue enhancements
**I**mproved management
**S**trategic positioning
**P**urchase price (must be less than value created!)

**Value created = Synergy value - Premium paid**
Most value often goes to selling shareholders!

sections.title: Deal Structures
sections.content:
**Payment methods:**

**Cash:** Simpler, but depletes resources
**Stock:** Shares dilution, but preserves cash
**Combination:** Balance of both

**Structure types:**

**Asset purchase:** Buy specific assets/liabilities
• Step-up basis for buyer
• Seller may have double taxation

**Stock purchase:** Buy shares of target
• Carryover basis
• Simpler but inherit all liabilities

sections.title: Acquisition Premium
sections.content:
**Premium = (Offer Price - Market Price) / Market Price**

**Example:**
Target trading at $40
Offer price: $52
Premium: ($52 - $40) / $40 = **30%**

**Why pay a premium?**
• Control value (can make changes)
• Synergy value
• Competition from other bidders

**Typical premiums: 20-40%**

**The acquirer must capture synergies > premium to create value!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Goodwill
sections.content:
**Purchase price exceeds fair value of net assets:**

**Goodwill = Purchase Price - FV of Net Identifiable Assets**

**Example:**
Purchase price: $100 million
FV of assets: $120 million
FV of liabilities: $40 million
Net identifiable assets: $80 million
Goodwill: $100M - $80M = **$20 million**

**Under GAAP, goodwill is NOT amortized but tested annually for impairment!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Horizontal M&A = same industry; Vertical = supply chain
Valuation: Comparables, Precedent transactions, DCF
Synergies: Cost (easier) and Revenue (harder)
Value created = Synergies - Premium paid
Payment: Cash depletes resources; Stock dilutes ownership
Asset purchase: step-up basis; Stock purchase: carryover
Goodwill = Price - FV of net identifiable assets (test for impairment)

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X is being acquired. An analyst uses Comparable Companies Analysis and Precedent Transactions to value Company X. The Comparable Companies Analysis yields a valuation range of $50 million to $60 million, while the Precedent Transactions method suggests a range of $65 million to $75 million. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the interpretation of these valuation ranges?
options: The Precedent Transactions method likely incorporates a control premium, which accounts for the higher valuation range compared to the Comparable Companies Analysis.
The Comparable Companies Analysis is inherently more accurate than the Precedent Transactions method due to its reliance on publicly traded companies.
The difference in valuation ranges indicates a fundamental flaw in one of the valuation methodologies, requiring it to be discarded.
Both methods should yield identical valuation ranges, and any discrepancy indicates an error in the data or calculations.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Precedent Transactions typically involve the acquisition of a controlling interest in a company, which includes a premium paid by the acquirer. This control premium results in higher valuations compared to Comparable Companies Analysis, which uses multiples based on minority stakes in publicly traded companies.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the type of transaction each valuation method is based on.

reveal.question: What are four examples of cost synergies typically pursued in M&A transactions?
reveal.answer: 1. Headcount reduction. 2. Facility consolidation. 3. Increased purchasing power. 4. Technology rationalization.

rows.label: Difficulty of Achievement
values: Generally Harder
Generally Easier

rows.label: Examples
values: Cross-selling, Geographic expansion
Headcount reduction, Facility consolidation

rows.label: Impact on Valuation
values: Potentially Higher, but more uncertain
More predictable and easier to quantify

title: International Finance
description: Manage foreign exchange risk and understand international financial concepts

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Global operations expose companies to foreign exchange risk that can wipe out operating profits overnight. CMAs must understand how exchange rates work, how to hedge currency exposure, and the parity conditions that drive international financial markets. This is an increasingly tested area on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Foreign Exchange Rate Quotations
sections.content:
**Direct Quote (American Terms):**
Domestic currency per unit of foreign currency
Example: $1.25/€1 (it costs $1.25 to buy one euro)

**Indirect Quote (European Terms):**
Foreign currency per unit of domestic currency
Example: €0.80/$1 (one dollar buys 0.80 euros)

**Key relationship:**
Direct quote = 1 / Indirect quote
$1.25/€ = 1 / (€0.80/$)

**Cross Rates:**
Derived from two quotes involving a common currency
If $1.25/€ and $1.50/£, then:
€ per £ = $1.50 / $1.25 = €1.20/£

**Currency Appreciation/Depreciation:**
• If rate goes from $1.25/€ to $1.30/€ → Euro appreciated (costs more dollars)
• If rate goes from $1.25/€ to $1.20/€ → Euro depreciated (costs fewer dollars)

**Tip:** In a direct quote, if the number goes UP, the foreign currency got STRONGER.

sections.title: Spot vs. Forward Rates
sections.content:
**Spot Rate:**
• Exchange rate for IMMEDIATE delivery (settlement in 2 business days)
• Reflects current supply and demand

**Forward Rate:**
• Exchange rate agreed TODAY for exchange at a FUTURE date
• Used to lock in exchange rates and hedge risk
• Common maturities: 30, 60, 90, 180 days, 1 year

**Forward Premium/Discount:**
Forward premium (%) = [(Forward - Spot) / Spot] × (360/days) × 100

**Example:**
Spot: $1.2500/€
90-day forward: $1.2600/€
Premium = [($1.2600 - $1.2500) / $1.2500] × (360/90) × 100
= (0.008) × 4 × 100 = **3.2% annualized premium**

**The euro is at a forward PREMIUM** (more expensive in the future).

**If forward < spot → Foreign currency is at a forward DISCOUNT**

sections.title: Interest Rate Parity (IRP)
sections.content:
**The forward rate reflects the interest rate differential between two countries.**

**Formula:**
Forward / Spot = (1 + Rdomestic) / (1 + Rforeign)

**Where R = interest rate for the period**

**Example:**
Spot: $1.2500/€
US 1-year rate: 5%
Eurozone 1-year rate: 3%

Forward = $1.2500 × (1.05/1.03) = $1.2500 × 1.0194 = **$1.2743/€**

**Interpretation:**
• Higher US interest rates → Dollar at forward discount
• The euro trades at a forward premium of ~1.94%
• This premium offsets the interest rate advantage

**Key Insight:**
IRP ensures you earn the same return whether you:
1. Invest domestically, OR
2. Convert to foreign currency, invest abroad, and hedge with a forward

**If IRP doesn't hold → Covered interest arbitrage opportunity exists!**

sections.title: Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)
sections.content:
**Exchange rates adjust to equalize purchasing power across countries.**

**Absolute PPP:**
A basket of goods should cost the same in all countries when converted to a common currency.
(Rarely holds due to trade barriers, transportation, non-traded goods)

**Relative PPP (More useful):**
Exchange rate changes reflect INFLATION rate differentials.

**Formula:**
(Expected Spot₁ - Spot₀) / Spot₀ ≈ Inflation_domestic - Inflation_foreign

**Example:**
Current spot: $1.2500/€
US expected inflation: 4%
Eurozone expected inflation: 2%

Expected change: 4% - 2% = 2%
Expected future spot: $1.2500 × 1.02 = **$1.2750/€**

**Interpretation:**
• Higher US inflation → Dollar weakens (depreciates)
• The currency of the higher-inflation country depreciates

**PPP explains long-run exchange rate trends but NOT short-run movements.**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: IRP vs. PPP
sections.content:
**"Interest drives FORWARDS, Prices drive SPOTS"**

**Interest Rate Parity (IRP):**
• Links interest rates to FORWARD rates
• Higher interest → Forward discount for that currency
• Holds well in practice (arbitrage enforces it)

**Purchasing Power Parity (PPP):**
• Links inflation to SPOT rate changes
• Higher inflation → Currency depreciates
• Works in long-run, not short-run

**Both say the same thing differently:**
The country with higher rates/inflation sees its currency weaken to offset the advantage.

sections.title: Transaction vs. Translation Exposure
sections.content:
**Transaction Exposure:**
• Risk from outstanding obligations in foreign currency
• Affects CASH FLOW
• Example: US company owes €1M to German supplier in 90 days
• If euro strengthens, the dollar cost INCREASES

**Hedging transaction exposure:**
1. **Forward contract** — Lock in the exchange rate today
2. **Money market hedge** — Borrow/invest to create offsetting position
3. **Currency options** — Buy the right (not obligation) to exchange
4. **Natural hedge** — Match foreign currency revenues with expenses

**Translation Exposure (Accounting Exposure):**
• Risk from converting foreign subsidiary financial statements to parent currency
• Affects REPORTED EARNINGS, not cash flow
• Under GAAP: Current rate method or temporal method

**Current Rate Method (most common):**
• Assets/Liabilities → Current exchange rate
• Equity → Historical rate
• Revenue/Expenses → Average rate
• Translation adjustment → Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)

**Economic Exposure:**
• Long-term impact of exchange rate changes on firm value
• Affects competitive position and future cash flows
• Hardest to measure and hedge

sections.title: Euromarkets
sections.content:
**Eurocurrency Market:**
• Deposits of a currency held OUTSIDE its home country
• Eurodollars = US dollars deposited outside the US (e.g., in London)
• Euroyen = Yen deposited outside Japan
• NOT related to the euro currency or Europe specifically!

**Why Euromarkets exist:**
• Less regulation than domestic markets
• Competitive interest rates (higher deposit, lower lending)
• Tax advantages in some jurisdictions
• 24-hour global access

**Eurobonds:**
• Bonds issued in a currency different from the country of issuance
• Example: A US company issues bonds denominated in yen in London
• Advantages: Avoid domestic registration, access global investors

**Foreign Bonds (contrast):**
• Issued in a foreign country, in THAT country's currency
• Yankee bonds: Foreign issuer, USD, in US market
• Samurai bonds: Foreign issuer, yen, in Japan
• Bulldog bonds: Foreign issuer, GBP, in UK

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Exchange Rate Direction
sections.content:
**The #1 source of errors on international finance questions:**

**Direct vs. Indirect quote confusion:**
• $1.25/€ (direct) — dollar cost of euros
• €0.80/$ (indirect) — how many euros per dollar

**Who benefits from what?**
• US EXPORTER benefits when dollar WEAKENS (foreign buyers get more for their money)
• US IMPORTER benefits when dollar STRENGTHENS (foreign goods are cheaper)

**Hedging direction:**
• If you OWE foreign currency → BUY it forward (lock in your cost)
• If you are OWED foreign currency → SELL it forward (lock in your revenue)

**Forward premium/discount shortcut:**
• Higher interest rate country → Currency at forward DISCOUNT
• Lower interest rate country → Currency at forward PREMIUM
• Remember: The forward rate OFFSETS the interest rate advantage

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Direct quote = domestic per foreign; Indirect = foreign per domestic
Forward rate locks in a future exchange rate — used for hedging
Interest Rate Parity: Forward rates reflect interest rate differentials
Purchasing Power Parity: Exchange rates adjust for inflation differences (long-run)
Transaction exposure affects cash flows; translation exposure affects reported earnings
Hedge transaction exposure with forwards, money market hedges, or options
Eurodollars are USD deposited outside the US — not related to the euro currency
Higher interest rate country's currency trades at a forward discount

knowledgeCheck.question: A U.S. company needs to pay a supplier €1,000,000 in 30 days. The current spot rate is $1.10/€ and the 30-day forward rate is $1.12/€. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate?
options: The euro is trading at a discount in the forward market, indicating lower interest rates in the Eurozone compared to the U.S.
The euro is trading at a premium in the forward market, indicating lower interest rates in the Eurozone compared to the U.S.
The dollar is trading at a premium in the forward market, indicating lower interest rates in the Eurozone compared to the U.S.
The dollar is trading at a discount in the forward market, indicating lower interest rates in the Eurozone compared to the U.S.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The forward rate ($1.12/€) is higher than the spot rate ($1.10/€), meaning the euro is more expensive in the future. This indicates the euro is trading at a premium. According to Interest Rate Parity, a currency trades at a premium in the forward market when the underlying country has lower interest rates. Therefore, the Eurozone has lower interest rates than the U.S.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the relative values of the spot and forward rates. Which currency costs more in the future?

reveal.question: What is the primary purpose of using a forward rate in international finance?
reveal.answer: The primary purpose of using a forward rate is to lock in a specific exchange rate for a future transaction, thereby hedging against exchange rate risk. This allows businesses to know exactly how much of their domestic currency will be needed to pay for foreign currency obligations or how much domestic currency they will receive from foreign currency revenues, eliminating uncertainty caused by fluctuating exchange rates.

knowledgeCheck.question: The spot rate between the British pound (£) and the U.S. dollar ($) is £0.80/$. The one-year interest rate in the UK is 4%, and the one-year interest rate in the U.S. is 6%. According to Interest Rate Parity, what is the approximate one-year forward rate between the pound and the dollar?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Using the IRP formula: Forward / Spot = (1 + Rdomestic) / (1 + Rforeign).  Here, the domestic currency is the pound.  So, Forward / 0.80 = (1 + 0.04) / (1 + 0.06).  Forward = 0.80 * (1.04/1.06) = approximately £0.784/$
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to correctly identify the domestic and foreign interest rates in the formula.

title: Short-Term Financing Options
description: Understand the various sources of short-term financing and their appropriate uses

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Even profitable companies need short-term financing to bridge timing gaps between cash outflows and inflows! CMAs help select the right financing sources based on cost, flexibility, and availability. Poor short-term financing decisions can be costly.

sections.title: Why Companies Need Short-Term Financing
sections.content:
**Working Capital Cycle:**
• Buy raw materials (cash out)
• Produce and hold inventory (cash tied up)
• Sell and create receivables (wait for payment)
• Finally collect cash (cash in)

**Gap to Fill:** Cash goes out before it comes back in

**Seasonal Needs:**
• Retailers build inventory before holidays
• Agricultural businesses have seasonal peaks

**Growth Financing:**
• Rapid growth requires more working capital
• Sales growth outpaces cash collection

sections.title: Sources of Short-Term Financing
rows: All companies; essentially free if no discount lost

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Short-Term Sources
sections.content:
**"TABLC" - From least to most formal:**

**T**rade credit - Supplier financing (informal)
**A**ccruals - Use wages/taxes timing (very short-term)
**B**ank credit - Lines and loans (formal)
**L**ending (asset-based) - Secured by current assets
**C**ommercial paper - Large company unsecured debt

Cost and formality generally increase as you go down!

sections.title: Trade Credit Cost
sections.content:
**Typical Terms:** "2/10, net 30"
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Otherwise, full amount due in 30 days

**Implicit Cost of Forgoing Discount:**

Annualized Cost = [Discount / (100 - Discount)] × [365 / (Full Days - Discount Days)]

**Example: 2/10, net 30**
= (2/98) × (365/20)
= 0.0204 × 18.25
= 37.2% annual cost!

**Decision Rule:** 
• If borrowing cost < 37.2%, borrow and take discount
• If borrowing cost > 37.2%, forgo discount (use trade credit)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Trade Credit Cost Formula
sections.content:
**The CMA exam frequently tests this calculation!**

**Formula:**
Cost = [Discount% / (100 - Discount%)] × [365 / (Net Days - Discount Days)]

**Common Mistake:** Using "Discount / 100" instead of "Discount / (100 - Discount)"

**Example: 3/15, net 45**
= (3/97) × (365/30)
= 0.0309 × 12.17
= 37.6%

**Key Insight:** Short discount periods and small day gaps = VERY expensive!

sections.title: Bank Lines of Credit
sections.content:
**Committed Line of Credit:**
• Bank guarantees funds will be available
• Commitment fee charged on unused portion (0.25-0.50%)
• More expensive but reliable

**Uncommitted Line of Credit:**
• Bank may decline to lend
• No commitment fee
• Less reliable during credit crunches

**Compensating Balance:**
• Required minimum balance (10-20% of line)
• Increases effective interest rate

**Effective Rate Example:**
$100,000 loan, 8% interest, 10% compensating balance
Usable funds: $90,000
Effective rate = $8,000 / $90,000 = 8.89%

sections.title: Commercial Paper
sections.content:
**Definition:** Short-term unsecured promissory notes issued by large corporations

**Characteristics:**
• Maturity: 1-270 days (typically under 90)
• Sold at discount from face value
• Minimum denominations: $100,000+
• Requires high credit rating (A-1/P-1)

**Advantages:**
• Lower cost than bank loans (no intermediary)
• Flexible amounts and maturities
• Standardized market

**Disadvantages:**
• Only available to large, creditworthy issuers
• Must maintain backup line of credit
• Market can dry up in crisis

sections.title: Factoring Receivables
sections.content:
**Definition:** Selling accounts receivable to a third party (factor) at a discount

**Types:**
• **Recourse:** Seller retains credit risk
• **Non-recourse:** Factor assumes credit risk (more expensive)

**Benefits:**
• Immediate cash
• Outsource credit and collection
• Off-balance sheet (if non-recourse)

**Costs:**
• Discount fee (1-5% of face value)
• Interest on advance
• May signal financial weakness

**Example:**
$1,000,000 receivables factored at 3% discount
Cash received: $970,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Short-term financing bridges timing gaps in the working capital cycle
Trade credit is common but forgoing discounts is expensive (often 30%+)
Cost formula: [Disc/(100-Disc)] × [365/(Net-Disc Days)]
Committed lines of credit charge fees but guarantee availability
Compensating balances increase effective interest rates
Commercial paper offers low rates but requires high credit rating
Factoring provides quick cash but at a cost

knowledgeCheck.question: A supplier offers terms of 2/15, net 45. What is the approximate annualized cost of forgoing the cash discount?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cost = (2/98) × (365/30) = 0.0204 × 12.17 = 24.8%. The company is effectively paying 24.8% annually to delay payment 30 days (from day 15 to day 45).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use the formula: [Discount/(100-Discount)] × [365/(Net Days - Discount Days)].

knowledgeCheck.question: A company borrows $200,000 at 9% interest with a 20% compensating balance requirement. What is the effective interest rate?
knowledgeCheck.hint: The compensating balance reduces usable funds but interest is paid on the full amount.

reveal.question: What are the typical requirements for a company to issue commercial paper?
reveal.answer: To issue commercial paper, a company typically needs: (1) High credit rating (A-1/P-1), (2) Established reputation in the market, (3) Backup line of credit from a bank (to ensure redemption), (4) Financial strength to support unsecured borrowing, and (5) Financing needs large enough to justify market access costs.

title: Cash Management and Treasury Operations
description: Optimize cash holdings and manage liquidity for organizational efficiency

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cash is the lifeblood of business! Holding too much cash means missed investment returns; holding too little risks liquidity crises. CMAs help optimize the cash position, implement collection strategies, and manage banking relationships effectively.

sections.title: Cash Management Objectives
sections.content:
**Primary Goals:**

**1. Ensure Adequate Liquidity**
• Meet obligations as they come due
• Maintain safety buffer

**2. Minimize Idle Cash**
• Invest excess cash productively
• Avoid opportunity cost of cash sitting unused

**3. Accelerate Collections**
• Get cash in quickly
• Reduce days sales outstanding (DSO)

**4. Control Disbursements**
• Pay on time but not early
• Maximize days payable outstanding (DPO)

sections.title: Understanding Float
sections.content:
**Float = Time between when you pay/receive and when bank processes**

**Collection Float (we want to minimize):**
• Mail float - check in transit
• Processing float - time to deposit
• Availability float - bank clearing time

**Disbursement Float (we may use strategically):**
• Time between writing check and bank debit
• Can manage payment timing

**Example:**
Write check Monday, mailed Tuesday
Received Thursday, deposited Friday
Clears Monday

**Total float: 7 days**
(You have use of money for 7 extra days)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Float Management
sections.content:
**"SLAM" - Reduce Collection Float:**

**S**peed up mail (lockbox near customers)
**L**ockbox processing (bank handles)
**A**utomate with ACH/wire
**M**obilize deposits immediately

**For Disbursements - opposite!**
Slow down (legally and ethically) to keep cash longer.

sections.title: Cash Concentration
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Move cash from multiple accounts to central account

**Benefits:**
• Better control and visibility
• More efficient investment
• Reduced banking costs
• Easier cash forecasting

**Methods:**

**Depository Transfer Checks (DTC)**
• Paper-based, takes 1-2 days
• Low cost, slower

**ACH Transfers**
• Electronic, 1-day settlement
• Low cost, faster

**Wire Transfers**
• Same-day value
• Higher cost, fastest

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Lockbox Economics
sections.content:
**Is the lockbox worth it?**

**Calculate the benefit:**

Daily collections × Days saved × Interest rate = Benefit

**Example:**
• Average daily collections: $100,000
• Float reduction: 3 days
• Annual opportunity cost: 6%

**Annual benefit:**
$100,000 × 3 × 6% = $18,000

**Decision:**
If lockbox costs < $18,000/year → Implement
If lockbox costs > $18,000/year → Don't

**Exam likely tests this calculation!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash management balances liquidity needs with minimizing idle cash
Float is the time between payment and bank processing
Collection strategies: lockbox, concentration, ACH, wire
Minimize collection float; may use disbursement float strategically
Lockbox value = Collections × Days saved × Interest rate
Cash concentration provides better control and investment opportunities
Treasury function coordinates all cash management activities

knowledgeCheck.question: A company with average daily collections of $200,000 is considering a lockbox that would reduce float by 2 days. If the company's opportunity cost is 5%, what is the maximum annual fee the company should pay for the lockbox?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Annual benefit = Daily collections × Days saved × Opportunity cost = $200,000 × 2 × 5% = $20,000. The company should pay no more than $20,000 annually for the lockbox to make it worthwhile.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the value of having money 2 days sooner on average.

title: Working Capital Metrics and Analysis
description: Calculate and interpret key ratios for receivables, inventory, and payables management

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital metrics reveal how efficiently a company manages its operating cycle! These ratios are HEAVILY TESTED on the CMA exam and essential for liquidity analysis, cash flow forecasting, and operational improvement initiatives.

sections.title: Accounts Receivable Metrics
sections.content:
**Receivables Turnover:**

Receivables Turnover = Credit Sales / Average A/R

**Example:**
Credit sales: $2,400,000
Average A/R: $200,000

Turnover = $2,400,000 / $200,000 = 12 times

**Days Sales Outstanding (DSO):**

DSO = 365 / Receivables Turnover

DSO = 365 / 12 = 30.4 days

**Interpretation:**
• Higher turnover = faster collections
• Lower DSO = better liquidity
• Compare to credit terms (if terms are 30, DSO of 30 is good)

sections.title: Inventory Metrics
sections.content:
**Inventory Turnover:**

Inventory Turnover = COGS / Average Inventory

**Example:**
COGS: $1,800,000
Average inventory: $300,000

Turnover = $1,800,000 / $300,000 = 6 times

**Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO):**

DIO = 365 / Inventory Turnover

DIO = 365 / 6 = 60.8 days

**Interpretation:**
• Higher turnover = inventory moving faster
• Lower DIO = less cash tied up
• But watch out: too low may mean stockouts!

sections.title: Accounts Payable Metrics
sections.content:
**Payables Turnover:**

Payables Turnover = Purchases / Average A/P

**Example:**
Purchases: $1,500,000
Average A/P: $125,000

Turnover = $1,500,000 / $125,000 = 12 times

**Days Payable Outstanding (DPO):**

DPO = 365 / Payables Turnover

DPO = 365 / 12 = 30.4 days

**Interpretation:**
• Lower turnover = paying slower (more float)
• Higher DPO = keeping cash longer
• But watch credit terms to avoid late fees!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Cash Conversion Cycle
sections.content:
**"DIO + DSO - DPO" = Cash Conversion Cycle**

(Days Inventory) + (Days Receivables) - (Days Payables)

**Example:**
DIO: 60 days
DSO: 30 days
DPO: 30 days

CCC = 60 + 30 - 30 = 60 days

**Meaning:** 60 days from paying for inventory to collecting cash from customers

**Goal:** Lower CCC = Less cash tied up = Better!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Numerator/Denominator Watch
sections.content:
**Use the RIGHT numerator:**

• **A/R Turnover:** Use CREDIT SALES (not total sales)
• **Inventory Turnover:** Use COGS (not sales)
• **A/P Turnover:** Use PURCHASES (not COGS)

**Use AVERAGE balances:**
• (Beginning + Ending) / 2
• Or use ending if only one balance given

**Exam loves these formula traps!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: A/R turnover = Credit Sales / Avg A/R; DSO = 365 / Turnover
Inventory turnover = COGS / Avg Inventory; DIO = 365 / Turnover
A/P turnover = Purchases / Avg A/P; DPO = 365 / Turnover
Cash Conversion Cycle = DIO + DSO - DPO
Lower CCC means less cash tied up in working capital
Use correct numerators: credit sales, COGS, purchases
Compare ratios to industry benchmarks and credit terms

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has: Days Inventory Outstanding = 45 days, Days Sales Outstanding = 35 days, Days Payable Outstanding = 40 days. What is the Cash Conversion Cycle?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO = 45 + 35 - 40 = 40 days. This means the company takes 40 days from paying for inventory until collecting cash from customers.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Add days to convert inventory, add days to collect, subtract days of supplier financing.

title: Trade Credit and Accounts Payable Management
description: Optimize supplier payment timing to balance cost savings and cash flow

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Trade credit is often the largest source of short-term financing! Understanding when to take discounts vs. preserve cash is a critical treasury decision. CMAs analyze the cost of foregoing discounts to determine optimal payment strategy.

sections.title: Understanding Credit Terms
sections.content:
**Standard Format: X/Y, Net Z**

**Example: 2/10, Net 30**
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Full amount due in 30 days
• Can keep money 20 extra days by skipping discount

**Components:**
• **Discount percentage:** 2%
• **Discount period:** 10 days
• **Net period:** 30 days
• **Extra days by foregoing:** 30 - 10 = 20 days

sections.title: Cost of Forgoing Discount
sections.content:
**The Key Formula:**

**Annualized Cost = [Discount % / (100% - Discount %)] × [365 / (Net Days - Discount Days)]**

**Example: 2/10, Net 30**

Cost = [2% / 98%] × [365 / 20]
Cost = 0.0204 × 18.25
Cost = 37.2% annually!

**Interpretation:**
Skipping the 2% discount costs 37.2% on an annualized basis
This is the implicit interest rate of supplier financing

**Decision Rule:**
If borrowing cost < 37.2%, take the discount (borrow to pay early)
If borrowing cost > 37.2%, skip the discount (use supplier financing)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Discount Formula
sections.content:
**Break it into two parts:**

**Part 1: Discount / (100 - Discount)**
• What you pay for keeping the money
• 2% discount = 2/98 = 2.04%

**Part 2: 365 / Extra Days**
• How many times per year
• 20 extra days = 365/20 = 18.25 times

**Annualize:** Multiply the parts!
2.04% × 18.25 = 37.2%

sections.title: Stretching Payables
sections.content:
**Paying beyond normal terms:**

**Potential Benefits:**
• Free financing (beyond net period)
• Preserves cash
• Lowers cost of foregoing discount

**Serious Risks:**
• Supplier relationships damaged
• Credit rating impact
• Late fees and penalties
• Supplier may refuse to ship
• Lose access to future discounts

**Best Practice:**
• Pay on last possible day (but not late)
• Take discounts if cost of capital permits
• Maintain good supplier relationships

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Formula Variations
sections.content:
**Watch for these exam variations:**

**Variation 1:** Using 360 days instead of 365
• Common in banking/finance questions
• Will slightly change result

**Variation 2:** Simplified formula
• Some texts use: (Discount/Net Amount) × (365/Extra Days)
• Same result, different presentation

**Variation 3:** Compare to bank rate
• Given bank loan at X%, should you take discount?
• Compare calculated cost to X%

**Key: Read the question carefully for which formula or days to use!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit terms: X/Y, Net Z (discount/discount period/net period)
Cost of forgoing = [Disc/(100-Disc)] × [365/(Net-Disc days)]
Typical early payment discounts have high annualized costs
Take discount if borrowing cost < cost of foregoing
Stretching payables damages relationships and credit
Pay on last day to maximize float without late payment
Compare implicit cost to available financing alternatives

knowledgeCheck.question: A supplier offers terms of 3/15, Net 45. What is the approximate annualized cost of foregoing the discount?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cost = [3/97] × [365/(45-15)] = (0.0309) × (365/30) = 0.0309 × 12.17 = 37.6%. Foregoing the 3% discount for 30 extra days costs 37.6% annually.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use the formula: [Disc%/(100-Disc%)] × [365/(Net days - Discount days)]

title: Banking Relationships and Treasury Management
description: Manage relationships with financial institutions to optimize cash services and financing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Banking relationships affect borrowing costs, cash efficiency, and access to credit! CMAs help evaluate bank service fees, negotiate terms, and determine the true cost of banking arrangements including compensating balance requirements.

sections.title: Bank Services for Businesses
sections.content:
**Cash Management Services:**
• Lockbox services (accelerate collections)
• Zero-balance accounts (concentrate cash)
• Controlled disbursement (predictable outflows)
• Wire transfers and ACH
• Positive pay (fraud protection)

**Credit Services:**
• Lines of credit
• Revolving credit facilities
• Term loans
• Letters of credit

**Other Services:**
• Payroll processing
• Foreign exchange
• Investment sweep accounts

sections.title: Compensating Balances
sections.content:
**Definition:** Minimum balance required to maintain as condition of loan or services

**Example:**
$1,000,000 credit line
10% compensating balance required
$100,000 must stay on deposit

**True Usable Credit:** $900,000

**Impact on Effective Interest Rate:**
Stated rate: 8%
Interest paid: $1,000,000 × 8% = $80,000
Usable funds: $900,000

**Effective Rate = $80,000 / $900,000 = 8.89%**
