**Key Point:** Compensating balances INCREASE effective borrowing cost!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Effective Rate
sections.content:
**Compensating Balance Adjustment:**

**Effective Rate = Stated Rate / (1 - Comp Balance %)**

**Example:**
Stated rate: 8%
Compensating balance: 10%

Effective Rate = 8% / (1 - 0.10) = 8% / 0.90 = 8.89%

**Higher comp balance = Higher effective rate**

sections.title: Bank Charges and Fees
sections.content:
**Two Payment Methods:**

**1. Direct Fees (Cash Payment)**
• Pay explicit charges for services
• Transparent pricing
• Example: $0.15 per check processed

**2. Compensating Balances**
• Maintain minimum balance instead of fees
• Bank earns interest spread on balance
• Less transparent

**Analysis:**
• Compare cost of leaving cash idle (opportunity cost)
• vs. direct fee payment
• Choose lower cost option

**Earnings Credit Rate (ECR):**
• Rate bank credits for balances
• Offsets service charges

sections.title: Line of Credit Arrangements
sections.content:
**Uncommitted Line:**
• Bank can refuse to lend
• Lower fees
• Less certainty

**Committed Line:**
• Bank obligated to lend (if terms met)
• Commitment fee on unused portion
• More certainty but higher cost

**Revolving Credit:**
• Formal committed facility
• Typically 3-5 year term
• Can borrow, repay, reborrow
• Often includes financial covenants

**Total Cost:**
= Interest on borrowed amounts
+ Commitment fee on unused amounts
+ Any compensating balance cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Total Borrowing Cost
sections.content:
**Calculate ALL costs:**

**Example:**
$5M revolving credit
6% interest on borrowed amounts
0.5% commitment fee on unused
Borrow average of $3M

**Interest:** $3M × 6% = $180,000
**Commitment Fee:** $2M × 0.5% = $10,000
**Total Cost:** $190,000

**Effective Rate:** $190,000 / $3,000,000 = 6.33%

**Don't forget:** Compensating balances or upfront fees!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Banks provide both cash management and credit services
Compensating balances increase effective borrowing cost
Effective rate = Stated rate / (1 - Comp balance %)
Compare direct fees vs compensating balance cost
Committed lines have fees but guaranteed access
Total cost includes interest + commitment fees + balance costs
Negotiate based on relationship value, not just one product

knowledgeCheck.question: A bank offers a $500,000 loan at 9% interest with a 15% compensating balance requirement. The company holds no other deposits. What is the effective interest rate?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Effective rate = Stated rate / (1 - CB%) = 9% / (1 - 0.15) = 9% / 0.85 = 10.59%. The company only has use of $425,000 (500,000 × 85%) but pays interest on the full amount.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Adjust for the portion of the loan that's locked up in compensating balances.

title: Accounts Receivable Management
description: Optimize credit policies, collection procedures, and receivables financing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Receivables are money waiting to be collected! Poor A/R management ties up capital and increases bad debt losses. CMAs analyze credit policies, monitor collection metrics, and evaluate financing options to optimize the cash-to-cash cycle.

sections.title: Credit Policy Components
sections.content:
**Credit Standards:**
• Who gets credit?
• Minimum credit score, ratios
• Tighter = fewer sales, less bad debt
• Looser = more sales, more bad debt

**Credit Terms:**
• Payment period (Net 30, Net 60)
• Early payment discounts (2/10, Net 30)

**Collection Procedures:**
• When to follow up
• Escalation process
• Write-off policies

**Credit Analysis Process:**
• Application and approval
• Credit limit determination
• Ongoing monitoring

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Five Cs of Credit
sections.content:
**Evaluate creditworthiness:**

**C**haracter - Will they pay? (History, reputation)
**C**apacity - Can they pay? (Cash flow)
**C**apital - Do they have resources? (Net worth)
**C**ollateral - What secures the debt?
**C**onditions - Economic/industry environment

**All five matter - don't rely on just one!

sections.title: Credit Policy Change Analysis
sections.content:
**Framework for evaluating credit policy changes:**

**Benefits:**
+ Incremental sales revenue
+ Incremental contribution margin

**Costs:**
- Higher bad debt expense
- Higher collection costs
- Opportunity cost of additional A/R

**Example:**
Extend credit terms from Net 30 to Net 45

**Projected:**
+ Sales increase: $200,000 (at 40% CM = $80,000)
- Additional bad debts: $10,000
- Additional A/R carrying cost: $15,000

**Net benefit: $80,000 - $25,000 = $55,000**

sections.title: Receivables Financing Options
sections.content:
**Factoring:**
• Sell receivables to factor (financing company)
• Get cash immediately (less fee)
• Factor collects from customers
• With recourse (you bear bad debt risk)
• Without recourse (factor bears risk)

**Asset-Based Lending:**
• Borrow against A/R as collateral
• Typically 70-80% of A/R balance
• You still collect and bear risk

**When useful:**
• Fast growth outpacing cash flow
• Seasonal businesses
• Need to accelerate cash

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cost of Factoring
sections.content:
**Factoring has explicit and implicit costs:**

**Explicit Costs:**
• Factor fee (1-5% of invoice face)
• Interest on advance

**What you give up:**
• Discount if factor collects early
• Potential customer relationship issues

**Example:**
$100,000 invoice
Factor fee: 2%
Advance: 90%
Interest on advance: 12% annually for 30 days

Cost = $2,000 + ($90,000 × 12% × 30/365) = $2,000 + $888 = $2,888

**Annualized cost is HIGH for short periods!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit policy: standards, terms, collection procedures
Tighter credit = less bad debt but fewer sales
Five Cs: Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions
Analyze credit changes with incremental cost-benefit
Factoring provides quick cash but has significant costs
With recourse: you bear bad debt risk; without: factor does
Monitor DSO and aging schedules to track performance

knowledgeCheck.question: A company loosens credit standards, expecting: +$150,000 new sales (30% contribution margin), +$12,000 bad debts, +$8,000 carrying costs. Should they make the change?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Incremental CM: $150,000 × 30% = $45,000. Incremental costs: $12,000 + $8,000 = $20,000. Net benefit: $45,000 - $20,000 = $25,000 positive. The change should be made.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate incremental contribution margin (not sales), then subtract incremental costs.

title: Inventory Management Systems
description: Compare EOQ, JIT, and modern inventory management approaches

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Inventory is expensive - too much ties up cash, too little means lost sales! CMAs analyze holding costs, ordering costs, and stockout risks to determine optimal inventory policies. EOQ calculations appear frequently on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Inventory Cost Components
sections.content:
**Holding (Carrying) Costs:**
• Storage space and utilities
• Insurance and taxes
• Obsolescence and spoilage
• Opportunity cost of capital
• Typically 20-30% of item cost annually

**Ordering Costs:**
• Purchase order processing
• Shipping and receiving
• Setup costs (for manufacturing)
• Quality inspection

**Stockout Costs:**
• Lost sales and margin
• Customer ill will
• Expediting costs
• Production disruption

sections.title: EOQ Model
sections.content:
**Economic Order Quantity Formula:**

EOQ = √ (2 × D × O / H)

**Where:**
• D = Annual demand (units)
• O = Ordering cost per order ($)
• H = Holding cost per unit per year ($)

**Example:**
D = 10,000 units/year
O = $50/order
H = $2/unit/year

EOQ = √ (2 × 10,000 × 50 / 2)
EOQ = √ 500,000
EOQ = **707 units**

**Number of orders = 10,000 / 707 ≈ 14 orders/year**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EOQ Assumptions
sections.content:
**EOQ assumes "CCCLD":**

**C**onstant demand (no seasonality)
**C**onstant costs (ordering and holding)
**C**omplete replenishment (instant delivery)
**L**ead time known
**D**emand independent (not linked to other products)

**When assumptions fail:**
• Variable demand → Safety stock
• Long lead time → Reorder point
• Quantity discounts → Compare total costs

sections.title: Reorder Point and Safety Stock
sections.content:
**Reorder Point (ROP):**

ROP = Daily Demand × Lead Time + Safety Stock

**Example:**
Daily demand = 50 units
Lead time = 5 days
Safety stock = 100 units

ROP = 50 × 5 + 100 = **350 units**

**Safety Stock Factors:**
• Demand variability
• Lead time variability
• Service level target
• Stockout cost

**Higher safety stock:**
+ Fewer stockouts
- Higher carrying costs

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Total Inventory Cost
sections.content:
**Know how to calculate total annual cost:**

**Total Cost = Purchase + Ordering + Holding**

**Ordering Cost** = (D/Q) × O
(Number of orders × Cost per order)

**Holding Cost** = (Q/2) × H
(Average inventory × Cost per unit)

**At EOQ:**
Ordering cost = Holding cost
(This is the insight of EOQ!)

**With Quantity Discounts:**
Compare total costs at:
• EOQ quantity
• Each price break quantity
• Choose lowest total cost

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three cost types: Holding, Ordering, Stockout
EOQ formula: √(2DO/H) minimizes total inventory costs
At EOQ, ordering costs = holding costs
ROP = (Daily Demand × Lead Time) + Safety Stock
EOQ assumptions: Constant demand/costs, instant delivery
JIT seeks zero inventory by reducing setup costs and variability
Quantity discounts require comparing total costs, not just unit price

knowledgeCheck.question: Annual demand is 4,000 units, ordering cost is $100 per order, and holding cost is $8 per unit per year. What is the EOQ?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Apply the EOQ formula: √(2DO/H) where D=demand, O=ordering cost, H=holding cost.

title: Short-Term Financing Instruments
description: Compare commercial paper, lines of credit, and other short-term funding sources

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Every company needs short-term funding! CMAs evaluate financing options, compare costs, and ensure liquidity. Understanding the range of instruments helps optimize the cost of capital while maintaining flexibility.

sections.title: Short-Term Financing Overview
sections.content:
**Spontaneous Financing:**
• Accounts payable - Trade credit from suppliers
• Accruals - Wages, taxes owed but not yet paid
• "Free" if paid on time

**Negotiated Financing:**
• Bank loans - Term loans, lines of credit
• Commercial paper - Direct market access
• Factoring - Sell receivables
• Asset-based lending - Borrow against assets

**Matching Principle:**
Fund temporary needs with short-term debt
Fund permanent needs with long-term debt

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Line of Credit Types
sections.content:
**Bank Credit Lines:**

**Uncommitted Line:**
• Bank MAY lend, no guarantee
• No commitment fee
• Can be revoked anytime

**Committed (Revolving) Line:**
• Bank MUST lend up to limit
• Pay commitment fee (0.25-0.50%)
• Contractual guarantee
• Draw and repay as needed

**Costs:**
• Interest on amounts borrowed
• Commitment fee on unused portion
• Sometimes: Compensating balance required

sections.title: Commercial Paper
sections.content:
**Definition:**
• Unsecured short-term promissory notes
• Issued directly in money market
• Typically 1-270 days maturity

**Advantages:**
• Lower rates than bank loans
• No compensating balance
• Large amounts available

**Disadvantages:**
• Only investment-grade companies
• Must maintain backup credit line
• Market access can disappear

**Backup Line:**
Funding insurance - if CP market closes,
draw on bank line instead
Critical for maintaining CP program!

sections.title: Cost Comparison Calculation
sections.content:
**Effective Annual Rate Formula:**

**Bank Loan with Compensating Balance:**
Effective Rate = Interest / (Loan - Balance)

**Example:**
$100,000 loan at 8%
10% compensating balance required ($10,000)
Usable funds: $90,000

Effective Rate = $8,000 / $90,000 = **8.89%**

**Discount Loan:**
Interest deducted upfront
Usable: $100,000 - $8,000 = $92,000

Effective Rate = $8,000 / $92,000 = **8.70%**

**Both increase effective cost above stated rate!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cost of Trade Credit
sections.content:
**Forgoing discount is EXPENSIVE:**

**Terms: 2/10, Net 30**
• 2% discount if paid in 10 days
• Otherwise pay full in 30 days

**Cost of Forgoing:**
Discount % / (100% - Discount %) × 365 / (Pay Date - Discount Date)

= 2/98 × 365/20
= 0.0204 × 18.25
= **37.2% annually!**

**Key Insight:**
Always take discounts if borrowing cost < 37%
Which is almost always!

**When to forgo:**
Only if company cannot borrow at all

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Spontaneous financing: Trade credit, accruals (automatic)
Negotiated: Bank loans, lines of credit, commercial paper
Committed line: Bank must lend; Uncommitted: Bank may lend
Commercial paper: Low cost but requires investment-grade rating
Compensating balances increase effective borrowing rate
Cost of forgoing trade discount is often extremely high (37%+ annualized)
Match financing term to asset/need duration

knowledgeCheck.question: A supplier offers terms of 3/15, Net 45. If the company forgoes the discount, what is the approximate annualized cost?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cost = (3/97) × (365/30) = 3.09% × 12.17 = 37.6% approximately. The 30 days comes from the difference between 45 days full payment and 15 days discount period.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Formula: (Discount / (100-Discount)) × (365 / (Pay Date - Discount Date))

title: Cash Budget Preparation
description: Build comprehensive cash budgets to manage liquidity and plan financing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: The cash budget is the MOST important short-term planning tool! It shows when cash shortages will occur and when excess cash needs investing. CMAs prepare cash budgets to ensure liquidity and minimize borrowing costs.

sections.title: Cash Budget Structure
sections.content:
**Beginning Cash Balance**
+ Cash Receipts
  - Collections from customers
  - Other cash income
= Cash Available

- Cash Disbursements
  - Payments to suppliers
  - Payroll
  - Other operating payments
  - Capital expenditures
  - Debt service
  - Dividends
= Ending Cash Before Financing

+/- Financing (borrowing or repayment)
= Ending Cash Balance

**Goal:** Maintain minimum cash balance

sections.title: Cash Receipts Calculation
sections.content:
**Most receipts come from sales collections:**

**Collection Pattern Example:**
• 20% collected in month of sale
• 50% collected in following month
• 25% collected in second month
• 5% uncollectible

**Example (Sales: Jan $100K, Feb $120K, Mar $150K):**

March Collections:
• March sales: $150,000 × 20% = $30,000
• February sales: $120,000 × 50% = $60,000
• January sales: $100,000 × 25% = $25,000
• **Total March collections: $115,000**

**Key:** Need several months' sales to calculate!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cash Budget Components
sections.content:
**"PRIMO" - What goes in cash budget:**

**P**urchases/Payables
• Raw materials, inventory
• When paid (lag from purchase)

**R**eceipts from customers
• Collection pattern key

**I**nvestments (capital expenditures)
• Equipment, property

**M**inimum cash balance
• Target safety buffer
• Determines financing needs

**O**perating expenses
• Payroll, rent, utilities
• When paid, not accrued

sections.title: Cash Budget Example
sections.content:
**March Cash Budget:**

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Beginning cash | $25,000 |
| + Collections | $115,000 |
| = Cash available | $140,000 |
| - Material purchases | ($60,000) |
| - Payroll | ($35,000) |
| - Operating expenses | ($20,000) |
| - Equipment purchase | ($40,000) |
| = Cash before financing | ($15,000) |
| Minimum required | $20,000 |
| **Financing needed** | **$35,000** |
| = Ending cash | $20,000 |

**Insight:** Need to borrow $35,000 in March

sections.title: Financing Section
sections.content:
**When Cash < Minimum:**
• Borrow from line of credit
• Round up to convenient amounts
• Plan for interest payments

**When Cash > Needs:**
• Repay borrowings
• Invest excess in short-term securities

**Financing Timing:**
• Assume borrowing at start of month
• Repayment at end of month
• Interest on borrowings

**Rolling 12-Month Budget:**
• See seasonal patterns
• Plan borrowing needs
• Negotiate credit lines

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Timing of Cash Flows
sections.content:
**Cash budget uses CASH flows, not accruals:**

**Purchases vs Payments:**
• If payment terms are Net 30...
• January purchases paid in February
• Must track purchase timing

**Depreciation:**
• NEVER in cash budget
• It's non-cash!

**Prepaid Expenses:**
• Cash goes out when PAID
• Not when expense recognized

**Common Errors:**
• Using sales instead of collections
• Using COGS instead of cash payments
• Including depreciation
• Wrong month for cash flows

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash budget: Beginning + Receipts - Disbursements = Ending
Collection patterns determine timing of cash from sales
Payment patterns determine timing of cash for purchases
Maintain minimum cash balance - difference drives financing
Exclude depreciation and non-cash items
Use CASH timing, not accrual timing
Cash budget reveals seasonal financing needs

knowledgeCheck.question: Sales are $80,000 (Jan), $90,000 (Feb), $100,000 (Mar). Collection pattern: 30% in month of sale, 60% next month, 10% two months later. What are March collections?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: March collections: March sales ($100K × 30% = $30K) + February sales ($90K × 60% = $54K) + January sales ($80K × 10% = $8K) = $30K + $54K + $8K = $92K. Correct answer should be $92,000, which is option B.
knowledgeCheck.hint: March collections come from three months: 30% of March, 60% of February, 10% of January.

title: International Cash Management
description: Manage cash across borders, currencies, and banking systems

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Global operations mean global cash! CMAs at multinational companies must manage cash across countries, currencies, and banking systems. International treasury adds complexity but also optimization opportunities through cash pooling and netting.

sections.title: International Cash Challenges
sections.content:
**Currency Issues:**
• Multiple currencies
• Exchange rate volatility
• Conversion costs
• Hedging needs

**Banking Complexity:**
• Multiple banks per country
• Different banking systems
• Time zone differences
• Varying technology

**Regulatory Constraints:**
• Capital controls
• Repatriation restrictions
• Tax implications
• Local reserve requirements

**Visibility:**
• Fragmented accounts
• Delayed reporting
• Manual reconciliation

sections.title: Cash Concentration Structures
rows: Actual cash movement to central account

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Netting Center
sections.content:
**Multilateral Netting:**

**Problem:** 10 subsidiaries paying each other
• Without netting: 90 possible payments
• High FX costs, bank fees

**Solution: Netting Center**
1. Each sub reports payables/receivables
2. Center calculates net position
3. Single payment per subsidiary

**Example:**
• Sub A owes Sub B: $100
• Sub B owes Sub A: $70
• Without netting: 2 payments ($170)
• With netting: 1 payment ($30)

**Savings:**
• Fewer transactions
• Lower FX costs
• Reduced bank fees
• Better visibility

sections.title: Payment Factory Concept
sections.content:
**What Is It?**
Centralized payment processing center
• All payments routed through one unit
• On behalf of (OBO) payments

**Benefits:**
• Standardized processes
• Bulk payments
• Better bank negotiation
• Fraud prevention
• Payment visibility

**In-House Bank:**
• Internal bank for subsidiaries
• Intercompany loans
• FX services
• Payment services
• Reduces external banking

sections.title: Currency Management
sections.content:
**Natural Hedging:**
• Match currency revenues and costs
• Produce where you sell
• Borrow in operating currencies

**Leading and Lagging:**
• Leading: Pay early if currency weakening
• Lagging: Pay later if currency strengthening
• Timing of intercompany payments

**Currency Netting:**
• Offset inflows and outflows
• Reduces FX transaction volume

**Reinvoicing Center:**
• Central entity handles all FX
• Subsidiaries transact in local currency
• Centralizes FX exposure

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Trapped Cash
sections.content:
**Some cash can't be moved!**

**Causes of Trapped Cash:**
• Capital controls (China, India, Brazil)
• Regulatory approval needed
• Tax cost of repatriation
• Thin capitalization rules
• Local statutory requirements

**Solutions:**
• Use cash locally (expansion, acquisitions)
• Intercompany loans (with limits)
• Transfer pricing (careful!)
• Dividends (with tax planning)
• Management fees, royalties

**CMA Alert:**
Reported cash may not be available cash
Always note restricted cash separately!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: International treasure adds currency, banking, and regulatory complexity
Physical pooling: Actual cash movement to central account
Notional pooling: Offset balances without moving cash
Netting: Settle intercompany amounts with single net payment
Payment factory: Centralize payment processing
In-house bank: Internal banking for subsidiaries
Trapped cash: Regulatory or tax restrictions on moving cash

knowledgeCheck.question: A structure where subsidiary account balances are offset for interest calculation purposes but cash is not physically moved is called:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Notional pooling offsets balances mathematically without moving cash. Subsidiaries maintain their local accounts, but the bank calculates interest on the net position. This avoids cash movement costs and legal/tax issues while achieving interest optimization.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which pooling method doesn't require actual cash transfers?

title: Float Management
description: Optimize cash through management of payment and collection timing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Float is 'free money' - if you manage it well! Float represents the time between when you write a check and when it clears. CMAs optimize float to reduce borrowing needs and improve cash positions. As payments become electronic, float management evolves but remains relevant.

sections.title: What is Float?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
The time delay between payment initiation and fund availability

**Collection Float:**
• Time until YOU receive funds
• Want to MINIMIZE
• Reduces your available cash

**Disbursement Float:**
• Time before funds LEAVE your account
• Want to MAXIMIZE (ethically)
• Extends your available cash

**Net Float:**
= Disbursement Float - Collection Float
• Positive = You have extra cash
• Negative = You're short cash

**Book Balance vs Bank Balance:**
Difference caused by outstanding checks/deposits

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Collection Techniques
sections.content:
**Reduce Collection Float:**

**Lockbox:**
• Bank PO box near customers
• Bank processes payments
• Reduces mail and processing time

**Concentration Banking:**
• Multiple collection points
• Sweep to central account
• Regional lockboxes

**Electronic Payments:**
• ACH, wire transfers
• Eliminate mail float
• Same-day availability

**Preauthorized Debits:**
• Auto-debit customer accounts
• Predictable cash flow
• No collection effort

sections.title: Disbursement Techniques
sections.content:
**Controlled Disbursement:**
• Bank notifies of daily clearings
• Fund account only as needed
• Maximize investment time

**Zero-Balance Accounts:**
• Operating accounts start at zero
• Fund from master as checks clear
• Concentration of funds

**Remote Disbursement:**
• Pay from distant banks
• Extends clearing time
• Less common now (ethics concerns)

**Payment Timing:**
• Pay on due date, not early
• Use full payment terms
• Consider early payment discounts

sections.title: Float Calculation
sections.content:
**Average Daily Float:**
= Average check amount × Collection days

**Annual Float Cost/Benefit:**
= Average daily float × Interest rate

**Example:**
Average daily collections: $100,000
Collection float: 3 days
Interest rate: 5%

Float = $100,000 × 3 = $300,000
Annual cost = $300,000 × 5% = $15,000

**If lockbox reduces to 1 day:**
New float = $100,000 × 1 = $100,000
Savings = ($300K - $100K) × 5% = $10,000

Compare to lockbox cost to decide

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Electronic Payments
sections.content:
**Float is shrinking with electronic payments!**

**Impact:**
• ACH/wire reduces float to near zero
• Check usage declining
• Real-time payments emerging

**What doesn't change:**
• Cash forecasting importance
• Timing of payments for cash flow
• Payment term optimization
• Balance visibility needs

**New Focus Areas:**
• Bank fee optimization
• Payment method selection
• Working capital financing
• Dynamic discounting

**Understand traditional float AND modern alternatives

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Float: Time between payment initiation and fund movement
Collection float: Minimize (faster receipts)
Disbursement float: Maximize ethically (slower payments)
Components: Mail, processing, availability float
Collection tools: Lockbox, concentration banking, electronic
Disbursement: Controlled disbursement, ZBAs, timing
Electronic payments reduce traditional float opportunities

knowledgeCheck.question: A company collects $500,000 per day. A lockbox would reduce collection float from 4 days to 1.5 days. At 6% annual interest, what is the maximum annual cost the company should pay for the lockbox?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Float reduction = 4 - 1.5 = 2.5 days. Cash freed = $500,000 × 2.5 = $1,250,000. Annual benefit = $1,250,000 × 6% = $75,000. The lockbox should cost less than $75,000 to be worthwhile.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the value of cash freed up × interest rate = maximum cost.

title: Working Capital Metrics
description: Measure and optimize working capital performance

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Working capital efficiency directly impacts cash flow! CMAs track working capital metrics to identify improvement opportunities and optimize the cash tied up in operations. Even profitable companies struggle when cash is trapped in working capital.

sections.title: Working Capital Defined
sections.content:
**Working Capital:**
Current Assets - Current Liabilities

**Operating Working Capital:**
(Receivables + Inventory) - Payables

**What It Represents:**
• Cash invested in operations
• Funds needed to bridge cash cycle
• Day-to-day liquidity

**Goal:**
• Minimize without hurting operations
• Balance liquidity and profitability
• Free up cash for other uses

**Key Insight:**
Working capital is an investment - it should earn a return!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cash Conversion Cycle
sections.content:
**CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO**

**Think of it as:**
1. Buy inventory (clock starts)
2. Hold inventory (DIO days)
3. Sell on credit (DSO more days)
4. Minus: Time before paying supplier (DPO)

**Example:**
• DIO: 45 days (inventory sitting)
• DSO: 35 days (waiting for customer)
• DPO: 30 days (before paying supplier)

CCC = 45 + 35 - 30 = **50 days**

Cash is tied up for 50 days!

**Optimization Target:**
• Reduce inventory (lower DIO)
• Collect faster (lower DSO)
• Negotiate terms (higher DPO)

sections.title: Key Working Capital Ratios
sections.content:
**Liquidity Ratios:**
• Current Ratio = CA / CL (benchmark: 1.5-2.0)
• Quick Ratio = (CA - Inventory) / CL (benchmark: 1.0)
• Cash Ratio = Cash / CL

**Activity Ratios:**
• Inventory Turnover = COGS / Avg Inventory
• Receivables Turnover = Revenue / Avg A/R
• Payables Turnover = COGS / Avg A/P

**Conversion:**
Days = 365 / Turnover Ratio

**Industry Comparison:**
• Benchmarks vary by industry
• Compare to peers
• Track trends over time

sections.title: Working Capital Optimization
sections.content:
**Receivables:**
• Credit policy optimization
• Early payment discounts
• Collection process
• Factoring/securitization

**Inventory:**
• Demand forecasting
• JIT/lean practices
• Safety stock optimization
• SKU rationalization

**Payables:**
• Negotiate better terms
• Don't pay early (unless discount)
• Supply chain financing
• Payment term optimization

**Balance:**
• Don't stretch suppliers to damage relationships
• Don't reduce inventory to harm service
• Don't tighten credit to lose customers

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: CCC Calculation
sections.content:
**Watch the denominators!**

**DIO and DPO:**
Use COGS in denominator
(Related to inventory/purchasing)

**DSO:**
Use Revenue in denominator
(Related to sales)

**Average vs Ending:**
• Preferred: Average balances
• Sometimes given: Ending balances
• Read carefully what's provided

**Negative CCC:**
• Possible! (e.g., Dell, Amazon)
• Collect from customers before paying suppliers
• Very efficient but rare

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Working capital = Current assets - Current liabilities
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (days cash is tied up)
DIO/DPO: Use COGS; DSO: Use Revenue
Lower CCC = Less cash invested in operations
Current ratio benchmark: 1.5-2.0; Quick ratio: 1.0
Optimization: Balance efficiency with operational needs
Track trends and compare to industry peers

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has: Inventory $200K, COGS $730K, A/R $150K, Revenue $1,095K, A/P $100K. What is the cash conversion cycle?
knowledgeCheck.hint: DIO and DPO use COGS; DSO uses Revenue. CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO.

title: Accounts Receivable Management
description: Optimize receivables through credit policy and collection management

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Receivables are a major investment - often 15-25% of assets! CMAs balance the sales benefits of offering credit against the costs of financing and bad debts. Effective A/R management directly improves cash flow and profitability.

sections.title: Credit Policy Components
sections.content:
**Credit Standards:**
• Who qualifies for credit?
• Credit scoring/analysis
• Tighter = fewer bad debts, maybe fewer sales

**Credit Terms:**
• Net 30, 2/10 net 30, etc.
• Payment timing and discounts

**Collection Policy:**
• How aggressively pursue collection?
• Follow-up procedures
• When to use collection agency?

**Trade-off:**
Liberal credit → More sales but more bad debts and financing
Tight credit → Fewer sales but less risk

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Credit Terms
sections.content:
**Understanding Credit Terms:**

**'2/10 net 30' means:**
• 2% discount if paid within 10 days
• Otherwise full amount due in 30 days

**Cost of Not Taking Discount:**

Formula:
(Discount % / 100 - Discount %) × (365 / Days lost)

**Example:**
2/10 net 30
• Discount: 2%
• Days lost: 30 - 10 = 20 days
• Cost: (2/98) × (365/20) = **37.2% annually!**

**Decision:**
Compare to borrowing cost - usually take discount!

sections.title: Receivables Metrics
sections.content:
**Days Sales Outstanding (DSO):**
= (Accounts Receivable / Credit Sales) × 365
• Shows average collection period
• Compare to credit terms

**Accounts Receivable Turnover:**
= Credit Sales / Average A/R
• How often A/R turns over
• Higher = faster collection

**Aging Schedule:**
• Breakdown by days outstanding
• Current, 30-60, 60-90, >90
• Identifies collection issues

**Bad Debt Expense:**
• % of sales method
• Aging analysis method

sections.title: Receivables Financing
sections.content:
**Factoring:**
• Sell receivables to factor
• Immediate cash (less discount)
• Factor bears collection risk (without recourse)
• Or you keep risk (with recourse)

**Asset-Based Lending:**
• Borrow against A/R as collateral
• Typically 70-80% advance rate
• You still collect

**Securitization:**
• Package and sell as securities
• Off-balance sheet (if qualified)
• Larger companies

**Trade-offs:**
• Cost vs speed of cash
• Control vs convenience

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Credit Policy Changes
sections.content:
**Analyzing Credit Policy Changes:**

**Compare incremental benefits vs costs:**

**Benefits:**
• Increased sales contribution margin
• Reduced financing cost (if tightening)

**Costs:**
• Increased bad debt expense
• Increased financing cost (A/R investment)
• Collection costs

**Required Calculation:**
Δ Profit = Δ CM - Δ Bad debts - Δ Financing cost

**Key Variables:**
• Current sales and expected change
• Contribution margin %
• Bad debt % (old vs new)
• DSO (old vs new)
• Cost of capital

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Credit policy: Standards, terms, collection procedures
5 C's: Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions
2/10 net 30: 2% discount if paid in 10 days, else due in 30
Cost of not taking discount = often 30%+ annually!
DSO: Average collection period; compare to credit terms
Factoring: Sell receivables for immediate cash
Evaluate credit changes: Δ CM vs Δ bad debts + Δ financing

knowledgeCheck.question: Credit terms are 3/15 net 45. What is the approximate annual cost of not taking the discount?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cost = (Discount / 100 - Discount) × (365 / Days lost) = (3/97) × (365/30) = 0.0309 × 12.17 = 37.6%. Days lost = 45 - 15 = 30 days. This represents the annualized cost of forgoing the discount.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Formula: (Discount% / (100-Discount%)) × (365 / Extra days)

title: Cash Management Strategies
description: Optimize cash position and liquidity

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cash is king - companies fail from lack of cash, not lack of profits! CMAs manage cash positions, forecast needs, and optimize liquidity. Effective cash management reduces borrowing costs and ensures the company can meet obligations. This is a core treasury function.

sections.title: Cash Management Objectives
sections.content:
**Primary Goals:**
• Ensure sufficient liquidity
• Minimize idle cash
• Maximize return on excess cash
• Minimize borrowing costs

**Balance Required:**
• Too much cash: Opportunity cost
• Too little cash: Liquidity risk, penalties

**Key Activities:**
• Cash forecasting
• Collections management
• Disbursements management
• Short-term investing
• Short-term borrowing
• Bank relationship management

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cash Forecasting
sections.content:
**Forecasting Approaches:**

**Receipts and Disbursements:**
• Detailed, short-term
• Daily/weekly cash flows
• Collection patterns
• Payment schedules

**Adjusted Net Income:**
• Start with projected income
• Adjust for non-cash items
• Adjust for timing differences
• More appropriate for longer term

**Key Collection Variables:**
• Credit terms offered
• Customer payment patterns
• Collection effort effectiveness

**Example:**
Sales: 30% cash, 40% collected M+1, 25% M+2, 5% bad debt

sections.title: Collections Acceleration
sections.content:
**Reduce Mailing Float:**
• Lockbox system
• Electronic payments
• Regional collection centers

**Reduce Processing Float:**
• Automated processing
• Remote deposit capture
• Same-day processing

**Reduce Availability Float:**
• Concentrate funds quickly
• Choose banks with fast clearing
• Electronic transfers

**Credit Management:**
• Evaluate customer creditworthiness
• Offer discounts for early payment
• Follow up on overdue accounts
• Balance sales vs bad debt risk

sections.title: Disbursement Management
sections.content:
**Timing:**
• Pay on due date (not early)
• Take discounts if beneficial
• Use appropriate payment methods

**Controlled Disbursement:**
• Know exact daily disbursement
• Minimize excess balances
• Fund accounts precisely

**Centralized Payables:**
• Consolidate payments
• Better control and visibility
• Negotiate better terms

**Electronic Payments:**
• ACH, wire transfers
• Predictable timing
• Audit trail
• Reduced check fraud

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cash Discount Decisions
sections.content:
**Should You Take the Discount?**

**Terms: 2/10, n/30**
• 2% discount if pay in 10 days
• Full amount due in 30 days
• Giving up discount = 20 days extra

**Cost of Not Taking Discount:**
= (Discount % / (100 - Discount %)) × (365 / Days difference)
= (2 / 98) × (365 / 20)
= 0.0204 × 18.25
= 37.2% annual cost!

**Decision:**
• If borrowing cost < 37.2%, TAKE discount
• Borrow to pay early

**Common Error:**
Calculating discount as simple 2% instead of annualized rate

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash management balances liquidity and return
Too much cash = opportunity cost; too little = risk
Forecast using receipts/disbursements or adjusted income
Accelerate collections: Lockboxes, electronic, early discount
Control disbursements: Pay on time, take discounts, centralize
Cash discounts have high annualized cost if not taken
Compare discount cost to borrowing costs

knowledgeCheck.question: Terms are 1/10, n/40. What is the approximate annualized cost of not taking the discount?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Cost = (1/99) × (365/30) = 0.0101 × 12.17 = 12.29%. Not taking the 1% discount costs about 12.3% annually. If you can borrow at less than 12.3%, you should take the discount.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use the formula: (Discount/(100-Discount)) × (365/Days difference)

title: Short-Term Financing Options
description: Understand sources of short-term borrowing

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Companies need short-term financing to bridge timing gaps! CMAs evaluate financing options, calculate costs, and recommend optimal sources. Understanding short-term financing is essential for working capital management and liquidity planning.

sections.title: Short-Term Financing Sources
sections.content:
**Spontaneous Sources:**
• **Trade credit:** Extended by suppliers
• **Accruals:** Wages, taxes payable
• Automatic, no formal arrangement

**Negotiated Sources:**
• **Bank loans:** Short-term borrowing
• **Lines of credit:** Pre-arranged borrowing
• **Commercial paper:** Market-based
• **Factoring:** Selling receivables
• Requires formal arrangement

**Secured vs Unsecured:**
• Secured: Backed by collateral (A/R, inventory)
• Unsecured: Based on creditworthiness

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Bank Borrowing
sections.content:
**Types of Bank Financing:**

**Single Loan:**
• One-time borrowing
• Fixed amount, fixed term

**Line of Credit:**
• Maximum borrowing limit
• Borrow as needed up to limit
• Uncommitted: Bank can refuse
• Committed: Bank must lend (for fee)

**Revolving Credit:**
• Like committed line
• Longer term (multi-year)
• Pay commitment fee on unused

**Cost Components:**
• Interest rate
• Commitment fee (on unused)
• Compensating balance requirement
• All affect effective rate!

sections.title: Compensating Balance
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Minimum balance required in bank account
Reduces usable funds from loan

**Effective Rate Impact:**

Effective rate = Interest / Net proceeds

**Example:**
• Borrow $100,000 at 8%
• 20% compensating balance
• Interest = $8,000
• Usable funds = $80,000

Effective rate = $8,000 / $80,000 = 10%

**If You Already Keep Balance:**
Compensating balance may not be incremental cost
Only calculate on ADDITIONAL funds tied up

sections.title: Commercial Paper
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Unsecured promissory note
Short-term (1-270 days typically)
Issued by large, creditworthy corporations

**Characteristics:**
• Sold at discount
• Low interest rate (below prime)
• Not backed by collateral
• Rated by agencies

**Cost calculation (discount basis):**
Effective rate = (Discount / Proceeds) × (360 / Days)

**Example:**
• $1M face value, 60 days
• Discount = $10,000
• Proceeds = $990,000

Rate = ($10,000 / $990,000) × (360/60) = 6.06%

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Effective Interest Rates
sections.content:
**Common Adjustments:**

**Discount Interest:**
Interest deducted upfront
Effective rate = Interest / (Principal - Interest)
• HIGHER than stated rate

**Compensating Balance:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Principal - Balance required)
• HIGHER than stated rate

**Both Together:**
Effective rate = Interest / (Principal - Interest - Balance)

**Common Mistake:**
Using face value as denominator

**Remember:**
Denominator = USABLE funds
Numerator = ACTUAL interest paid

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Spontaneous: Trade credit, accruals (automatic)
Negotiated: Bank loans, lines, commercial paper, factoring
Line of credit: Borrow up to limit as needed
Compensating balance raises effective rate
Discount interest raises effective rate
Commercial paper: Low cost, creditworthy issuers only
Effective rate = Interest / Usable proceeds

knowledgeCheck.question: A company borrows $500,000 at 9% for one year with a 10% compensating balance. What is the effective interest rate?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate interest, then divide by usable funds.

title: Inventory Management and EOQ
description: Optimize inventory levels and ordering decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Inventory ties up significant capital! CMAs analyze inventory costs to minimize total cost while maintaining service levels. Understanding EOQ, reorder points, and safety stock is essential for working capital optimization. This is heavily tested on the CMA exam.

sections.title: Inventory Costs
sections.content:
**Carrying (Holding) Costs:**
• Storage and warehousing
• Insurance
• Obsolescence/spoilage
• Opportunity cost of capital
• Handling costs

**Ordering Costs:**
• Purchase order processing
• Receiving and inspection
• Shipping charges
• Communications

**Stockout Costs:**
• Lost sales
• Customer dissatisfaction
• Production disruption
• Expediting costs

**Goal:** Minimize TOTAL cost

sections.title: EOQ Model
rows: Holding cost per unit per year

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EOQ Formula
sections.content:
**Economic Order Quantity:**

**EOQ = √(2DS / H)**

Where:
• D = Annual demand
• S = Cost per order (Setup)
• H = Holding cost per unit per year

**Example:**
• D = 10,000 units/year
• S = $50 per order
• H = $2 per unit per year

**EOQ = √(2 × 10,000 × 50 / 2)**
     = √(500,000)
     = 707 units

**Orders per year:** 10,000 / 707 = 14.1
**Average inventory:** 707 / 2 = 354 units

**Remember:** EOQ balances ordering and holding costs

sections.title: Reorder Point and Safety Stock
sections.content:
**Reorder Point (ROP):**
When to place an order

**Basic ROP (no safety stock):**
ROP = Lead time demand
ROP = Daily demand × Lead time days

**With Safety Stock:**
ROP = Lead time demand + Safety stock

**Safety Stock:**
• Buffer against uncertainty
• Demand variability
• Lead time variability
• Higher service level = More safety stock

**Example:**
• Daily demand: 100 units
• Lead time: 5 days
• Safety stock: 200 units
• ROP = (100 × 5) + 200 = 700 units

sections.title: EOQ Assumptions and Limitations
sections.content:
**EOQ Assumes:**
• Constant demand rate
• Fixed ordering cost
• Fixed holding cost
• Instantaneous replenishment
• No quantity discounts
• No stockouts

**Real World Issues:**
• Demand varies
• Quantity discounts may exist
• Lead time varies
• Costs may not be linear

**Extensions:**
• EOQ with quantity discounts
• Production EOQ (gradual replenishment)
• Stochastic models (uncertain demand)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EOQ Changes
sections.content:
**How Changes Affect EOQ:**

**If Demand (D) Increases:**
• EOQ increases (√D)
• But not proportionally
• Double D → EOQ × 1.41

**If Ordering Cost (S) Increases:**
• EOQ increases
• Order more per order (fewer orders)

**If Holding Cost (H) Increases:**
• EOQ decreases
• Order less per order (more orders)

**Common Exam Questions:**
• Calculate EOQ
• Calculate total cost at EOQ
• What happens if variable changes?
• Calculate reorder point

**Remember:**
At EOQ: Ordering cost = Holding cost
Total cost minimized

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EOQ = √(2DS/H) - minimizes total inventory cost
Ordering costs decrease with larger orders
Holding costs increase with larger orders
At EOQ: Order cost = Holding cost
ROP = Lead time demand + Safety stock
Safety stock protects against uncertainty
EOQ assumes constant demand and costs

knowledgeCheck.question: Annual demand is 8,000 units, ordering cost is $100 per order, and holding cost is $4 per unit per year. What is the EOQ?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: EOQ = √(2DS/H) = √(2 × 8,000 × 100 / 4) = √(400,000) = 632.5 ≈ 632 units. This order quantity minimizes total inventory costs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use the EOQ formula: √(2DS/H)

title: Factoring and Accounts Receivable Financing
description: Monetize receivables for improved cash flow

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Need cash but have receivables tied up? Factoring and A/R financing convert future collections into immediate funds. CMAs evaluate these options to optimize working capital and compare costs to other financing sources.

sections.title: A/R Financing Options
sections.content:
**Three Main Approaches:**

**1. Pledging (Secured Loan):**
• A/R as collateral for loan
• Company still collects
• Company retains risk
• Customers unaware

**2. Factoring (Assignment):**
• Sell A/R to factor
• Factor collects from customers
• Customers notified
• With or without recourse

**3. Securitization:**
• Package A/R into securities
• Sell to investors
• Used by large companies
• Complex structure

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Factoring Terms
sections.content:
**Key Factoring Concepts:**

**Factor:** Financial institution buying receivables

**Advance Rate:** % paid upfront (typically 80-90%)
• Get $80K upfront on $100K receivables
• Remainder when collected minus fees

**Factoring Fee:** Cost of the service (1-5%)
• Based on credit risk, industry, terms

**Reserve/Holdback:** Amount retained until collection
• Protects factor against returns, disputes

**Notification:** Whether customers know
• Notification: Factor collects directly
• Non-notification: Company still collects

**Recourse:** Who bears bad debt risk
• With recourse: Seller takes back bad debts
• Without: Factor absorbs losses

sections.title: Calculating Factoring Cost
sections.content:
**Example:**
$100,000 receivables factored
Advance rate: 85%
Factoring fee: 3%
Collection period: 30 days

**Immediate Cash:**
$100,000 × 85% = $85,000

**Factoring Fee:**
$100,000 × 3% = $3,000

**Net After Collection:**
$100,000 - $3,000 = $97,000
Less advance: - $85,000
Receive later: $12,000

**Annual Cost:**
$3,000 / $85,000 = 3.53%
Annualized: 3.53% × (365/30) = 43%

**Compare to:** Bank line of credit, other financing

sections.title: Benefits and Drawbacks
sections.content:
**Benefits:**
• Immediate cash access
• No additional debt on balance sheet (if sale)
• Outsource collections
• Credit expertise of factor
• Scales with sales

**Drawbacks:**
• Can be expensive
• Customer relationships (notification)
• May signal financial weakness
• Dependence on factor
• Recourse liability

**Best Suited For:**
• Fast-growing companies
• Seasonal businesses
• Limited credit history
• B2B with creditworthy customers

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Sale vs Borrowing
sections.content:
**Accounting Treatment:**

**Sale Treatment (Remove A/R):**
• Transfer risks and rewards
• Surrender control
• No continuing involvement
• Without recourse typically qualifies

**Borrowing Treatment (Keep A/R):**
• Recourse provisions
• Retained servicing
• Repurchase agreements
• Record A/R and liability

**Key Question:**
Who bears the risk of bad debts?
• Factor = Sale
• Seller = Borrowing

**Exam Tip:**
With recourse often treated as secured borrowing
Without recourse usually treated as sale

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Factoring: Sell receivables to factor for immediate cash
With recourse: Seller retains bad debt risk (cheaper)
Without recourse: Factor assumes risk (costlier)
Advance rate: % received upfront (80-90%)
Annualize factoring cost to compare alternatives
Sale vs borrowing depends on risk transfer
Good for fast growth, seasonal, limited credit

knowledgeCheck.question: A company factors $200,000 of receivables without recourse at 2% fee with 90% advance rate. How much cash is received immediately?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Immediate cash = $200,000 × 90% advance rate = $180,000. The 2% fee ($4,000) is deducted from the holdback amount, not the advance. After collection, the company receives: $200,000 - $180,000 advance - $4,000 fee = $16,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The advance rate determines immediate cash; fees are typically deducted later.

title: Lines of Credit and Bank Financing
description: Structure and manage bank credit facilities

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Bank financing is a core funding source! CMAs help negotiate credit facilities, monitor covenants, and ensure compliance. Understanding bank financing structures is essential for treasury and working capital management.

sections.title: Types of Bank Financing
sections.content:
**Short-Term Options:**
• **Line of Credit:** Pre-approved borrowing limit
• **Revolving Credit:** Borrow, repay, re-borrow
• **Commercial Paper:** For large, creditworthy companies
• **Banker's Acceptance:** Trade finance

**Term Loans:**
• Fixed amount, fixed term
• Regular principal + interest payments
• 1-7 years typically
• Can be secured or unsecured

**Asset-Based Lending:**
• Secured by A/R, inventory, equipment
• Borrowing base determined by collateral
• Higher monitoring, more flexibility

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Credit Facility Costs
sections.content:
**Total Cost of Credit Facility:**

**Interest Cost:**
• Prime + spread OR SOFR + spread
• Applied to borrowed amount
• May have floor rate

**Commitment Fee:**
• Fee on UNUSED portion of committed line
• Typically 0.25-0.50%
• Pays for guaranteed availability

**Facility Fee:**
• Fee on TOTAL line (used + unused)
• Less common

**Example:**
$10M committed line
Borrowed: $4M at 6%
Unused: $6M at 0.5% commitment fee

Interest: $4M × 6% = $240K
Commitment: $6M × 0.5% = $30K
Total cost: $270K

sections.title: Debt Covenants
sections.content:
**What are Covenants?**
• Promises borrower makes to lender
• Protect lender's interests
• Restrict borrower actions
• Violation = default

**Affirmative Covenants (Must Do):**
• Provide financial statements
• Maintain insurance
• Pay taxes
• Maintain assets

**Negative Covenants (Can't Do):**
• Limitations on additional debt
• Restrictions on dividends
• Limits on asset sales
• Restrictions on M&A

**Financial Covenants:**
• Maintain minimum ratios
• Debt-to-EBITDA, interest coverage
• Current ratio, net worth

sections.title: Managing Covenant Compliance
sections.content:
**CMA Role:**
• Monitor covenant ratios monthly
• Forecast future compliance
• Alert management to potential issues
• Negotiate covenant structure

**Covenant Violation Consequences:**
• Technical default
• Loan acceleration possible
• Higher interest rate
• Additional restrictions
• Amendment fee

**Prevention Strategies:**
• Build adequate cushion in ratios
• Negotiate realistic covenants upfront
• Model scenarios in forecasts
• Communicate early with lenders

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Effective Interest Rate
sections.content:
**Watch for Compensating Balances:**

**Compensating Balance:**
• Minimum deposit required at bank
• Reduces usable funds
• Increases effective interest rate

**Example:**
$100,000 loan at 6%
10% compensating balance required

Usable funds: $100K - $10K = $90K
Interest cost: $100K × 6% = $6K

Effective rate: $6K / $90K = 6.67%

**With Commitment Fee:**
$100K line, use $60K
6% interest, 0.5% commitment on unused

Interest: $60K × 6% = $3,600
Commitment: $40K × 0.5% = $200
Total: $3,800
Effective rate on borrowed: $3,800 / $60K = 6.33%

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Line of credit: Pre-approved borrowing limit
Uncommitted: Bank can refuse; Committed: Guaranteed
Commitment fee: Cost of unused portion
Covenants: Affirmative (must do), Negative (can't do), Financial (ratios)
Covenant violation = technical default
Compensating balances increase effective rate
CMAs monitor compliance and forecast covenant risk

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a $5 million committed line of credit with 0.4% commitment fee on unused amounts. If the company borrows $3 million, what is the annual commitment fee?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Commitment fee is charged on the UNUSED portion. Unused = $5M - $3M = $2M. Commitment fee = $2M × 0.4% = $8,000 per year.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate the unused portion first, then apply the fee percentage.
